MongoDB Atlas suits the document-oriented patron profile schema but Koha remains the source of truth for catalog items -- the API must not duplicate item records in MongoDB, only cache search results in Redis. The migration job must map legacy ILS patron barcodes to Firebase Auth UIDs before any data is surfaced in the app.
reasoner_reviews.security: Firebase Auth JWT validation in Express middleware is standard but the middleware must extract patron_id from the Firebase custom claims (not the sub claim) to ensure the patron_id matches the university's student ID system, not a Firebase-generated UID. Push notification payloads for due date reminders must not include catalog item titles in the lock screen notification body -- only 'You have items due soon' -- to avoid exposing patron borrowing records on the lock screen.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Koha REST API rate limits must be respected -- catalog searches should be routed through the Redis cache (5-min TTL) to avoid hammering Koha during peak hours. iOS APNs certificate management via Fastlane Match simplifies team credential sharing across multiple developers' Xcode environments.

frontend.app_structure: Flutter app with feature-first package structure: lib/features/catalog, lib/features/holds, lib/features/account, lib/features/notifications. Riverpod StateNotifier providers for catalog search results, active holds, and borrowing history.
frontend.pages_and_flows: Patron flows: home (search bar, due date banner), search results (title, author, branch availability), item detail (availability per branch, place hold button), holds list (pending/ready/expired), borrowed items (due dates, offline cached), account (profile, notification preferences). Library staff flow: hold queue browser by branch.
frontend.state_management: Riverpod for async server state (catalog search, holds, history); Hive boxes for offline borrowed items cache; flutter_local_notifications for in-app due date banners.
frontend.frontend_risks: Koha API response latency causing slow search results -- mitigated by Redis cache and optimistic UI with shimmer loading. iOS APNs certificate expiry causing push delivery failure -- mitigated by Fastlane Match cert rotation reminders.

backend.service_design: CatalogService: proxies Koha search and item availability API calls, caches results in Redis. HoldService: wraps Koha hold placement and cancellation API, polls for hold status transitions. PatronService: manages patron profile in MongoDB, borrowing history, migration mapping. NotificationService: schedules FCM push for due-date-3-days and hold-ready events via Cloud Tasks.
backend.api_patterns: GET /v1/catalog/search?q=&isbn= (Redis-cached Koha proxy), GET /v1/catalog/items/:id/availability, POST /v1/holds (place hold via Koha API), DELETE /v1/holds/:id (cancel), GET /v1/patrons/me/history, GET /v1/patrons/me/holds, POST /v1/patrons/me/notification-prefs
backend.background_jobs: Cloud Tasks queue: due-date-reminder tasks created when item checked out, executed 3 days before due date; hold-ready tasks created on Koha hold status webhook. Migration job: one-time Cloud Run job mapping legacy ILS patron barcodes to Firebase UIDs via student ID crosswalk table.
backend.failure_handling: Koha API unavailability returns stale Redis cache with 'availability may be delayed' banner; hold placement failures surface Koha error codes translated to patron-friendly messages; Cloud Tasks retry on FCM delivery failure up to 3 attempts

security.auth_design: Firebase Auth with university SAML SSO federation; custom claims set by Firebase Admin SDK on first login: {patron_id: university_student_id, role: 'patron'|'staff'}; JWT validated in Express middleware using firebase-admin SDK
security.authorization_model: Patron endpoints: Firebase JWT required, patron_id extracted from custom claims, all MongoDB queries filter by patron_id. Staff endpoints: role='staff' claim required, unrestricted hold queue read access.
security.privacy_controls: Push notification payloads: alert body limited to 'Items due in 3 days' and 'Your hold is ready for pickup' -- no item titles in payload. Patron borrowing history excluded from Google Cloud Monitoring log exports.
security.security_risks: Koha REST API credentials stored in Cloud Run secrets -- must rotate on staff departure. Firebase custom claims can be forged if Admin SDK is exposed -- Admin SDK must only run in backend, never in Flutter app.

data.entities: library_patrons (patron_id, firebase_uid, student_id, email, branch_preference, notification_prefs, migrated_at), catalog_items (cached from Koha, item_id, title, author, isbn, branch_locations_json, availability_last_checked), hold_requests (hold_id, patron_id, item_id, koha_hold_id, status ENUM pending/ready/expired/cancelled, placed_at, ready_at, pickup_branch), checkout_records (id, patron_id, item_id, koha_checkout_id, due_date, returned_at, source ENUM legacy/app), digital_licenses (id, patron_id, resource_id, checkout_expiry, provider), due_date_reminders (id, checkout_id, patron_id, scheduled_for, sent_at, channel ENUM fcm/apns)
data.storage_design: MongoDB Atlas (GCP us-central1): library_patrons, hold_requests, checkout_records collections. Redis (Cloud Memorystore): catalog search results keyed by normalized query hash, 5-min TTL; item availability keyed by item_id, 2-min TTL. Hive (on-device): borrowed items list for offline access, synced on app foreground.
data.migration_notes: Legacy ILS patron barcode to Firebase UID mapping via student_id crosswalk; borrowing history last 3 years migrated from Koha checkouts API into checkout_records collection; migration job idempotent with patron_id deduplication.

devops.deployment_topology: Cloud Run: library-api service (min 0, max 5 instances, 1vCPU/512MB); Cloud Tasks queue for notification scheduling; MongoDB Atlas M10 cluster (GCP us-central1); Redis Cloud Memorystore Basic; Firebase project for Auth, FCM, Crashlytics.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: Node.js tests (Jest, 80% coverage), ESLint, build and push to Artifact Registry, deploy Cloud Run. Flutter CI: flutter test, flutter build ios (Fastlane Match), flutter build apk, upload to TestFlight and Play Internal Track on main branch merge.
devops.observability_stack: Firebase Crashlytics: Flutter crash reports with stack traces. Google Cloud Monitoring: Cloud Run request count, latency, error rate. Custom Firestore analytics events: catalog_search, hold_placed, due_date_reminder_sent, item_due_date_extended.

target_output.thinking_summary: First-pass architecture for a Flutter library catalog mobile app migrating patrons from a desktop Koha ILS portal. Key decisions: Koha remains the source of truth for catalog items -- the backend only proxies and caches Koha responses in Redis rather than duplicating item records in MongoDB. The migration job must resolve legacy ILS patron barcodes to Firebase UIDs via student ID crosswalk before any patron data is accessible in the app. Push notifications must not expose item titles in lock screen payloads to protect patron borrowing privacy. Cloud Tasks handles asynchronous due-date and hold-ready notification scheduling.
target_output.title: University Library Mobile Catalog -- Flutter Migration Architecture
target_output.executive_summary: A Flutter 3 mobile app for iOS and Android migrating university library patrons from a desktop-only Koha ILS patron portal to a mobile-first catalog search, hold request, and due date reminder experience. A Node.js/Express API on Cloud Run proxies Koha catalog calls with Redis caching, manages patron profiles in MongoDB Atlas, and schedules push notifications via Cloud Tasks and Firebase Cloud Messaging. Legacy patron barcodes and borrowing history are migrated from Koha via a one-time migration job. FERPA-compliant patron data scoping enforced at the Firebase Auth JWT claim level.
target_output.architecture_overview: Flutter app communicates with a Node.js Express API on GCP Cloud Run behind a Cloud Endpoints gateway. CatalogService proxies Koha ILS REST API calls and caches search results in Cloud Memorystore Redis (5-min TTL). HoldService wraps Koha hold placement and polls for status transitions. PatronService stores patron profiles and migrated borrowing history in MongoDB Atlas. NotificationService enqueues Cloud Tasks for due-date (3-day) and hold-ready push notifications delivered via Firebase Cloud Messaging to iOS (APNs) and Android (FCM). Hive local storage on the device caches the borrowed items list for offline access. Firebase Auth handles authentication with university SAML SSO federation; custom claims carry patron_id and role.
target_output.technology_stack: Mobile: Flutter 3 with Dart, Riverpod state management, flutter_hooks, Hive for local storage, flutter_local_notifications, firebase_messaging plugin. Backend: Node.js 20, Express, firebase-admin SDK for JWT validation, axios for Koha REST API calls. Database: MongoDB Atlas 7 (GCP us-central1), Redis 7 on Cloud Memorystore. Auth: Firebase Auth with university SAML SSO. Push: Firebase Cloud Messaging (Android FCM + iOS APNs). Hosting: Cloud Run (min 0, max 5), Cloud Tasks. CI/CD: GitHub Actions, Fastlane for iOS code signing and App Store submission, Fastlane Match for certificate management.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Catalog Search: keyword, author, ISBN queries proxied through Redis-cached Koha API; branch availability per campus location. Hold Requests: place and cancel holds via Koha API; holds list showing pending/ready/expired status. Borrowing History: migrated from legacy ILS plus new checkouts; due date display with traffic-light urgency indicator. Offline Borrowed Items: Hive-cached list available without network connectivity; synced on app foreground. Push Notifications: 3-days-before-due reminder and hold-ready pickup alert via FCM/APNs; lock screen body does not include item title. Patron Account: notification preferences, branch preference, migration status. Library Staff View: hold queue by pickup branch.
target_output.system_components: Cloud Run library-api service: CatalogService, HoldService, PatronService, NotificationService modules. Cloud Tasks queue: due-date-reminder and hold-ready notification task execution. MongoDB Atlas M10 cluster: library_patrons, hold_requests, checkout_records, digital_licenses collections. Cloud Memorystore Redis: catalog search result and item availability cache. Firebase project: Auth (SAML SSO federation), FCM (push delivery), Crashlytics (mobile crash reporting). Koha ILS: external source of truth for catalog items and authoritative hold/checkout state. One-time migration Cloud Run job: patron barcode-to-Firebase UID crosswalk and borrowing history import.
target_output.workflows: Catalog Search and Hold Placement: patron enters search query in Flutter app; app calls GET /v1/catalog/search?q=organic+chemistry; API checks Redis cache for normalized query hash; cache miss proxies to Koha REST API GET /api/v1/biblios?q=...; results cached for 5 minutes; patron taps item, views branch availability (2-min Redis TTL from Koha item endpoint); taps 'Place Hold', app calls POST /v1/holds; HoldService calls Koha POST /api/v1/holds, receives koha_hold_id; hold_request inserted into MongoDB with status=pending; Cloud Tasks poll job checks Koha hold status every 30 min; when Koha status transitions to 'waiting', hold_request.status updated to 'ready' and hold-ready Cloud Task fired to deliver FCM push notification. Legacy ILS Migration: one-time Cloud Run migration job reads Koha patrons API for all active patrons, matches student_id against university directory crosswalk, creates library_patrons MongoDB document with firebase_uid, imports last 3 years of checkout records from Koha checkouts API into checkout_records collection; migration_status flag on library_patrons set to 'completed'; app checks flag on first login and shows migration confirmation banner.
target_output.data_model: library_patrons (patron_id: string, firebase_uid: string, student_id: string, email: string, branch_preference: string, notification_prefs: {due_reminder: bool, hold_ready: bool}, migrated_at: Date). hold_requests (hold_id: ObjectId, patron_id: string, item_id: string, koha_hold_id: int, status: enum pending/ready/expired/cancelled, placed_at: Date, ready_at: Date, pickup_branch: string, expiry_date: Date). checkout_records (id: ObjectId, patron_id: string, item_id: string, koha_checkout_id: int, title_cached: string, due_date: Date, returned_at: Date, source: enum legacy/app). digital_licenses (id: ObjectId, patron_id: string, resource_id: string, checkout_expiry: Date, provider: string). due_date_reminders (id: ObjectId, checkout_id: ObjectId, patron_id: string, scheduled_for: Date, sent_at: Date, channel: enum fcm/apns). branch_locations (branch_id: string, name: string, address: string, hours_json: object).
target_output.api_design: GET /v1/catalog/search?q=&isbn=&author= (Redis-cached Koha proxy, JWT optional for unauthenticated browse). GET /v1/catalog/items/:id/availability (2-min Redis TTL, Koha branch stock). POST /v1/holds (body: {item_id, pickup_branch}, requires JWT patron claim). DELETE /v1/holds/:koha_hold_id (requires JWT, validates patron_id ownership). GET /v1/patrons/me/holds (JWT required, returns MongoDB hold_requests for patron_id). GET /v1/patrons/me/history?limit=50 (JWT required, checkout_records). PATCH /v1/patrons/me/notification-prefs (JWT required). POST /internal/notifications/due-reminder (Cloud Tasks authenticated endpoint, not public). GET /health.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Firebase Auth: university SAML SSO federated; custom claims set by Admin SDK at first login: {patron_id: university_student_id, role: 'patron'|'staff'}. Express middleware validates Firebase JWT using firebase-admin SDK; extracts patron_id from custom claims (not sub) for all data access queries. MongoDB queries always filter by patron_id matching JWT claim -- no cross-patron access possible. Staff role allows hold queue read across all patrons. Push notification payloads: alert body is 'You have items due in 3 days' or 'Your hold is ready for pickup at [branch]' -- no item title or author exposed in lock screen payload. FERPA: borrowing history access restricted to the patron and authorized staff; analytics events use anonymized patron tokens after 6 months; Cloud Run logs exclude patron_id from structured log fields. Koha API credentials stored in Cloud Run Secret Manager.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Cloud Run library-api: deployed via GitHub Actions on main branch merge; Docker image pushed to Artifact Registry; Cloud Run rolling update with 100% traffic shift after health check. Flutter CI: GitHub Actions runs flutter test and flutter analyze on PR; on main branch merge, Fastlane builds iOS IPA (Fastlane Match App Store profile) and Android APK; uploads to TestFlight and Play Console Internal Track for manual QA approval before production release. Migration job: one-time Cloud Run job triggered manually by library IT staff via gcloud run jobs execute; idempotent with patron_id deduplication; completion logged to Cloud Logging. Environments: dev (Cloud Run local emulator, MongoDB Atlas free tier, Koha sandbox), staging (Cloud Run, Atlas M0, Koha staging), production (Cloud Run min-instances=1 during library hours 08:00-22:00 via Cloud Scheduler, MongoDB Atlas M10).
target_output.observability: Firebase Crashlytics: Flutter crash reports with Dart stack traces, device model, and OS version. Google Cloud Monitoring: Cloud Run request count, p50/p99 latency, error rate 5xx. Custom analytics events via Firebase Analytics: catalog_search (query_length, result_count), hold_placed (branch, item_type), due_date_reminder_sent, hold_ready_notification_sent, migration_completed. Alerts: Cloud Run error rate >2% fires PagerDuty; Crashlytics crash-free rate <99% fires Slack notification; MongoDB Atlas disk >80% fires ops alert.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Cloud Run: scales to zero overnight; min-instances=1 during library hours via Cloud Scheduler; estimated 500 daily active patrons = ~$30/month. MongoDB Atlas M10: ~$57/month. Cloud Memorystore Redis Basic 1GB: ~$25/month. Firebase Auth, FCM, Crashlytics: free tier sufficient for single-institution pilot. Cloud Tasks: $0.40 per million tasks; 5,000 daily due-date tasks = ~$2/month. Total: ~$120/month.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 -- MVP Migration (7 weeks): Cloud Run library-api with CatalogService (Koha proxy + Redis cache), HoldService, PatronService, Firebase Auth with SAML SSO, Flutter app with catalog search, hold placement, borrowing history display, Hive offline cache for borrowed items, migration Cloud Run job for patron barcode crosswalk and 3-year checkout history import. Phase 2 -- Notifications (3 weeks): NotificationService with Cloud Tasks, FCM/APNs push notifications for due-date reminders and hold-ready alerts, patron notification preferences, staff hold queue view. Phase 3 -- Analytics and Polish (2 weeks): Firebase Analytics event instrumentation, instructor analytics (not applicable -- library staff hold queue analytics), digital license checkout via ILS API, Fastlane automated App Store and Play Store production release pipeline.
target_output.development_guidelines: Koha API calls must always go through CatalogService -- no direct Koha calls from HoldService or PatronService. Redis cache keys use SHA-256 hash of normalized query parameters. Push notification payloads reviewed against patron privacy checklist before FCM API call. Flutter app must pass flutter analyze --no-fatal-infos before PR merge. Migration job must be tested against Koha sandbox with 100 synthetic patron records before production run. Firebase custom claims set by Admin SDK only; never set from Flutter app code.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Koha REST API downtime causing catalog search failure -- mitigated by serving stale Redis cache with banner indicating real-time availability may be delayed. Risk: iOS APNs certificate expiry causing hold-ready notification failures during exam periods -- mitigated by Fastlane Match certificate renewal reminders in GitHub Actions and 30-day expiry alert. Tradeoff: MongoDB Atlas over PostgreSQL for patron profiles -- MongoDB's flexible schema suits patron profiles with variable ILS field mappings across Koha version upgrades, but lacks pgvector if recommendation features are added later. Tradeoff: Cloud Run scale-to-zero over always-on instance reduces cost but adds cold-start latency for first patron search of the day; min-instances=1 during library hours mitigates.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Migration scope: last 3 years of borrowing history migrated (not all-time) to limit migration job runtime and MongoDB storage. Offline cache: only borrowed items (not full catalog) cached locally -- catalog search requires connectivity. Koha hold polling interval: 30-minute Cloud Tasks poll chosen over Koha webhook (unavailable in current Koha version). Push payload privacy: item title excluded from lock screen payload; only branch name included in hold-ready alert.

metadata.notes: Flutter library catalog mobile app migrating patrons from desktop Koha ILS portal to mobile-first experience with FERPA-compliant patron data handling.

profile.domain: University IT Security / Network Device Audit CLI

project_goal.value: CLI tool for university IT security teams to audit campus network device configurations against CIS benchmark policies, detect misconfigurations in firewalls, switches, and access points, and generate prioritized remediation reports
project_goal.rationale: Manual audit processes are error-prone and slow; automated CIS benchmark checking reduces misconfiguration dwell time

target_users.value: University IT security administrators and network engineers
target_users.rationale: Small team of ~10 IT staff responsible for campus network security posture

project_class.rationale: Command-line invocation by IT staff; no browser UI needed; runs on IT workstations and jump boxes

capabilities.rationale: Go binary connects to network devices, evaluates configurations, stores findings in SQLite, integrates with network management systems

complexity_level.rationale: SSH and SNMP device connection protocols, CIS benchmark rule engine, SQLite persistence, and report generation are non-trivial but scoped

risk_level.rationale: Tool accesses sensitive network device credentials and configuration data; misuse could expose network topology to attackers

data_sensitivity.rationale: Network device configurations and topology data are confidential; exposure could facilitate targeted attacks on campus infrastructure

external_exposure.rationale: CLI runs exclusively on IT staff workstations within the campus management network; not exposed to internet

access_model.value: Custom JWT authentication for API integration endpoints; device credentials loaded from encrypted local keystore file or environment variables; no multi-user session management
access_model.rationale: CLI tool single-user model; device credentials must not be stored in plaintext

feature_scope.value: SSH and SNMP device connection, CIS benchmark rule evaluation per device type (Cisco IOS, Palo Alto firewall, Aruba AP), finding severity classification, SQLite persistence for trend analysis, HTML and JSON remediation report generation, scan comparison across runs
feature_scope.rationale: Complete audit lifecycle from device discovery through trend comparison

mvp_scope.value: SSH-based configuration pull for Cisco IOS switches, 50 CIS benchmark rules for Cisco IOS, finding severity classification, SQLite storage, and HTML remediation report
mvp_scope.rationale: Most campus devices are Cisco IOS; validates rule engine before expanding to Palo Alto and Aruba

security_baseline.value: Device credentials encrypted in local keystore (AES-256-GCM), SSH host key verification enforced, no telemetry transmitted outside campus network, SQLite database file encrypted at rest using SQLCipher
security_baseline.rationale: Network device credentials and topology data are confidential; local encryption prevents exposure if workstation is compromised

frontend_stack.value: None -- CLI tool; all output is stdout, stderr, and file-based reports (HTML, JSON)
frontend_stack.rationale: No browser or native UI; output consumed by IT staff directly or uploaded to compliance tracking systems

backend_stack.rationale: Go produces a single static binary for easy distribution to IT workstations; strong SSH and network library support; fast compilation

data_platform.value: SQLite via SQLCipher for encrypted local scan result storage; no remote database
data_platform.rationale: Local storage avoids network egress of confidential configuration data; SQLCipher provides encryption at rest

hosting_target.value: Self-hosted; distributed as a Go binary for Linux and macOS (university IT workstations and jump boxes)
hosting_target.rationale: No cloud hosting; binary deployed to IT staff machines directly

privacy_retention_policy.value: Scan results retained in local SQLite for 2 years for trend analysis; report files deleted after 30 days per IT policy; no scan data transmitted to external systems
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Confidential network topology data must not leave the campus management network

future_scope.value: Palo Alto firewall and Aruba AP benchmark support; CI/CD integration for automated weekly scans; SIEM integration for finding export to Splunk
future_scope.rationale: Expanding device coverage and automation after Cisco IOS MVP validates rule engine

constraints.value: Must run on Linux and macOS without root privileges; no external runtime dependencies (single static binary); scan execution must complete within 10 minutes for a 200-device campus network
constraints.rationale: IT staff workstations vary in OS; root requirement would block deployment; 10-min scan window fits maintenance window constraints

observability_baseline.value: Structured JSON logging to stdout with --log-level flag; scan progress bar; no remote monitoring
observability_baseline.rationale: IT staff monitor scan progress via terminal output; no remote telemetry needed for local CLI tool

execution_preference.value: GitHub Actions CI for build and release; goreleaser for multi-platform binary releases
execution_preference.rationale: goreleaser automates Linux/macOS binary builds and GitHub Releases with checksums

compliance_context.value: NIST 800-53 controls for federal research institutions receiving federal funding; GLBA Safeguards Rule for institutions handling student financial aid data on networked systems; CIS Benchmark profiles selected to align with NIST control families AC, IA, SC, SI
compliance_context.rationale: University receives federal research funding requiring NIST 800-53 compliance; GLBA applies due to student financial aid processing on campus network

project.goal: Go CLI tool for CIS benchmark audit of campus network devices
project.target_users: University IT security admins and network engineers
project.timeline: MVP in 6 weeks targeting Cisco IOS devices

backend.report: html/template for HTML reports, encoding/json for JSON output

security.credentials: AES-256-GCM encrypted keystore file or env vars
security.ssh: Host key verification enforced via known_hosts

devops.ci: GitHub Actions: go test, go vet, staticcheck, govulncheck
devops.release: goreleaser for linux/amd64, linux/arm64, darwin/amd64, darwin/arm64 binaries

constraints.binary: Single static binary, no external runtime deps
constraints.scan_time: 200 devices in <10 minutes via concurrent SSH goroutines

open_questions.concurrency: Max concurrent SSH connections to avoid overwhelming campus switches

reasoner_reviews.product: The CIS benchmark rule engine is the core value. The rule definitions should be data-driven (YAML or JSON files bundled into the binary) rather than hardcoded in Go so IT staff can update rules without recompiling. Trend comparison across scan runs (finding new misconfigurations and tracking remediation) is the feature that differentiates this from a one-shot scan script.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Go's goroutine model makes concurrent SSH connections natural -- a semaphore limiting concurrent connections to 20 will prevent overwhelming managed switches. The rule engine should use a visitor pattern over the parsed device config AST, allowing new device types to be added by implementing the DeviceParser interface. SQLCipher integration with Go requires CGO which complicates the single-static-binary constraint -- evaluate pure-Go SQLite encryption alternatives.
reasoner_reviews.security: The keystore encryption key must never be derived from the username or hostname alone -- use a passphrase-derived key via PBKDF2 with a per-keystore salt stored alongside the encrypted file. SSH host key verification must enforce strict mode equivalent to StrictHostKeyChecking=yes, rejecting unknown hosts. The tool should produce an integrity hash (SHA-256) of each report file so compliance auditors can verify reports were not tampered with after generation.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: The 10-minute scan window for 200 devices is achievable with 20 concurrent SSH goroutines (10s per device average). goreleaser's CGO_ENABLED=0 builds pure-Go binaries -- SQLCipher requires CGO. This is an architectural conflict that must be resolved before MVP.

backend.service_design: CLI command tree using cobra: 'netaudit scan --targets devices.yaml --profile cis-cisco-ios-v8', 'netaudit report --scan-id <id> --format html|json', 'netaudit compare --scan-id-a <id> --scan-id-b <id>', 'netaudit credentials add --device <host>'. ScanEngine orchestrates concurrent device connections and rule evaluation. RuleEngine loads CIS policy YAML files bundled in binary via embed.FS. ReportEngine renders HTML and JSON output from AuditFindings.
backend.api_patterns: No HTTP API -- all interaction via CLI flags and subcommands. cobra command tree with persistent flags: --db-path (SQLite file), --keystore-path, --log-level, --timeout, --max-concurrent (default 20).
backend.failure_handling: SSH connection failures logged with device hostname and error; scan continues to remaining devices; summary shows failed_devices count. SNMP timeout triggers fallback to SSH if both protocols configured for device. Device credential not found in keystore exits with error code 2 and instructive message.
backend.backend_risks: SQLCipher CGO requirement conflicts with single-static-binary constraint -- evaluate go-sqlcipher vs. pure-Go file-level encryption wrapper. Max concurrent SSH goroutines must be tuned to avoid switch CPU exhaustion during audit.

security.auth_design: Keystore file: AES-256-GCM encrypted JSON map of hostname to {username, password, ssh_key_path}; encryption key derived via PBKDF2-SHA256(passphrase, salt, 100000 iterations); salt stored as base64 in keystore header. CLI prompts for passphrase on first keystore access per invocation; cached in process memory only.
security.secrets_management: Keystore file permissions enforced at 0600 on creation; tool refuses to run if keystore permissions are world-readable. Env var fallback: NETAUDIT_DEVICE_USER, NETAUDIT_DEVICE_PASS for single-device scans. SSH private key paths stored in keystore, not key material.
security.audit_and_logging_controls: Each scan_run record includes operator_username (from OS user), scan_start, scan_end, device_count, finding_count. Report files include SHA-256 integrity hash in footer. JSON log entries include scan_run_id for correlation.
security.security_risks: Passphrase cached in process memory could be read from /proc/[pid]/mem on Linux by root -- mitigated by mlock on passphrase buffer and warning if running under sudo.

data.entities: network_devices (id, hostname, device_type ENUM cisco_ios/palo_alto/aruba_ap, ip_address, snmp_community_encrypted, last_scanned_at), configuration_snapshots (id, device_id, scan_run_id, config_text_hash, collected_at), cis_benchmark_policies (id, policy_id, device_type, rule_id, description, check_expression, severity ENUM critical/high/medium/low, cis_version), audit_findings (id, scan_run_id, device_id, policy_id, severity, status ENUM fail/pass/not_applicable, finding_detail, first_seen_at, last_seen_at), remediation_items (id, finding_id, recommended_command, cis_reference, nist_control_id), scan_runs (id, operator_username, scan_profile, started_at, finished_at, device_count, finding_count, db_path)
data.storage_design: Single SQLite file per operator workstation; SQLCipher AES-256 encryption with key derived from keystore passphrase. WAL mode for concurrent read performance during report generation.
data.migration_notes: Schema versioned via embedded migrations using golang-migrate; new rule sets added as INSERT-only migrations to cis_benchmark_policies table.

devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: go test ./... with -race flag, go vet, staticcheck, govulncheck for CVE scanning. goreleaser builds linux/amd64, linux/arm64, darwin/amd64, darwin/arm64 binaries on semver tag push; uploads to GitHub Releases with SHA-256 checksums file.
devops.environments: No deployment environments -- binary distributed to IT staff workstations via GitHub Releases download. Internal distribution channel: university IT staff Slack channel with release notification.
devops.devops_risks: CGO_ENABLED=0 required for pure-Go static binary is incompatible with SQLCipher (C library); must resolve before first release. govulncheck in CI catches CVEs in golang.org/x/crypto dependency.

target_output.thinking_summary: First-pass CLI architecture for a Go-based CIS benchmark audit tool for campus network devices. The critical architectural flaw is the conflict between the single-static-binary constraint (CGO_ENABLED=0) and SQLCipher which requires CGO -- the plan will proceed with SQLCipher but note the conflict without resolving it, making this an intentionally flawed plan. Additionally, the plan inconsistently includes a 'web dashboard' reference in the architecture overview despite the project being a cli_tool, and the security section underspecifies NIST 800-53 control family mapping required by the compliance_context contract field.
target_output.title: Campus Network Security Audit CLI -- CIS Benchmark Architecture
target_output.executive_summary: A Go 1.22 command-line tool for university IT security teams to audit campus network device configurations against CIS benchmark policies. The tool connects to Cisco IOS switches, Palo Alto firewalls, and Aruba access points via SSH and SNMP, evaluates configurations against 50+ CIS rules, stores findings in an encrypted SQLite database for trend analysis, and generates HTML and JSON remediation reports ranked by severity. A web dashboard is available for browsing historical scan results across the team.
target_output.architecture_overview: Single Go binary distributed to IT workstations. ScanEngine dispatches concurrent SSH goroutines (max 20 via semaphore) to pull running configurations from network devices. RuleEngine evaluates device configuration text against CIS benchmark YAML rule definitions embedded in the binary via embed.FS. AuditFindings are persisted to a SQLCipher-encrypted SQLite database on the operator's local filesystem. ReportEngine renders HTML and JSON reports from findings. A separate web dashboard process serves scan history to the broader IT team via HTTP on localhost:8080, though this conflicts with the CLI-only project architecture.
target_output.technology_stack: Language: Go 1.22 (CGO_ENABLED=1 for SQLCipher). CLI framework: cobra for subcommand tree. SSH: golang.org/x/crypto/ssh. SNMP: github.com/gosnmp/gosnmp. Database: SQLite via mattn/go-sqlite3 with SQLCipher extension. Report templates: html/template (HTML), encoding/json (JSON). Concurrency: sync.Semaphore for SSH connection limit. Embedding: embed.FS for CIS rule YAML files. Build: goreleaser. CI: GitHub Actions with govulncheck.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Scan Command: 'netaudit scan --targets devices.yaml --profile cis-cisco-ios-v8 --max-concurrent 20' -- connects to each device, pulls running config, evaluates CIS rules, persists findings to SQLite. Report Command: 'netaudit report --scan-id <id> --format html' -- queries findings, renders HTML report with findings sorted by severity, writes to ./reports/scan-<id>.html with SHA-256 footer hash. Compare Command: 'netaudit compare --scan-id-a <id> --scan-id-b <id>' -- diffs findings between two scans, shows new misconfigurations, resolved findings, and persisting violations. Credentials Subcommand: 'netaudit credentials add --device 10.1.1.1' -- encrypts device credentials into AES-256-GCM keystore. Web Dashboard: separate 'netaudit serve' subcommand launching HTTP server for team scan history browse.
target_output.system_components: netaudit binary: cobra command tree (scan, report, compare, credentials, serve). ScanEngine: goroutine pool with semaphore, dispatches DeviceConnector per target. DeviceConnector: SSH client using golang.org/x/crypto/ssh with strict host key checking; SNMP client via gosnmp. RuleEngine: loads CIS YAML rules via embed.FS, evaluates check_expressions against config text using a simple expression evaluator. ReportEngine: html/template and json marshaling. Keystore: AES-256-GCM encrypted JSON file with PBKDF2 key derivation. SQLite+SQLCipher: local encrypted database for scan_runs, audit_findings, network_devices. Web server: net/http serving scan history -- present despite cli_tool classification.
target_output.workflows: CIS Benchmark Scan: operator runs 'netaudit scan --targets campus-switches.yaml --profile cis-cisco-ios-v8'; ScanEngine reads devices from YAML, creates scan_run record, dispatches goroutines up to semaphore limit of 20; each goroutine: opens SSH connection to device (verifies host key against known_hosts file), executes 'show running-config', stores config_text_hash in configuration_snapshots, passes config text to RuleEngine; RuleEngine evaluates each cis_benchmark_policies rule against config text using check_expression (e.g., 'contains:"service password-encryption"'), inserts audit_finding with pass/fail/not_applicable status; on completion, updates scan_run with finding_count and duration; progress bar updates via terminal. Remediation Report Generation: operator runs 'netaudit report --scan-id 42 --format html'; ReportEngine queries audit_findings JOIN cis_benchmark_policies JOIN remediation_items for scan_run 42; renders HTML table sorted by severity DESC; appends SHA-256 hash of report content to file footer; writes to ./reports/scan-42.html.
target_output.data_model: network_devices (id INTEGER PK, hostname TEXT, device_type TEXT CHECK cisco_ios/palo_alto/aruba_ap, ip_address TEXT, last_scanned_at DATETIME). configuration_snapshots (id INTEGER PK, device_id FK, scan_run_id FK, config_text_hash TEXT, collected_at DATETIME). cis_benchmark_policies (id INTEGER PK, policy_id TEXT UNIQUE, device_type TEXT, rule_id TEXT, description TEXT, check_expression TEXT, severity TEXT, cis_version TEXT, nist_control_id TEXT). audit_findings (id INTEGER PK, scan_run_id FK, device_id FK, policy_id FK, severity TEXT, status TEXT, finding_detail TEXT, first_seen_at DATETIME, last_seen_at DATETIME). remediation_items (id INTEGER PK, finding_id FK, recommended_command TEXT, cis_reference TEXT, nist_control_id TEXT). scan_runs (id INTEGER PK, operator_username TEXT, scan_profile TEXT, started_at DATETIME, finished_at DATETIME, device_count INT, finding_count INT).
target_output.api_design: No HTTP API for core audit functionality -- all interaction via CLI subcommands and flags. 'netaudit scan --targets <file> --profile <profile> [--max-concurrent N] [--timeout 30s] [--db-path ~/.netaudit/scans.db]'. 'netaudit report --scan-id <id> --format html|json [--output-dir ./reports]'. 'netaudit compare --scan-id-a <id> --scan-id-b <id> [--format html|json]'. 'netaudit credentials add|list|remove --device <hostname>'. 'netaudit serve --port 8080 [--db-path]' -- starts HTTP server for web dashboard (architectural inconsistency: web server in CLI tool). Exit codes: 0 success, 1 scan errors, 2 credential not found, 3 database error.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Keystore: AES-256-GCM encrypted JSON; key derived via PBKDF2-SHA256 with per-file salt and 100,000 iterations; passphrase prompted on first use per process, held in mlock'd buffer. SSH: strict host key checking against ~/.netaudit/known_hosts; tool exits with error on unknown host fingerprint; no auto-add-to-known-hosts option exposed. SQLCipher: database encrypted with key derived from keystore passphrase; database file permissions set to 0600. Report integrity: SHA-256 hash appended to report files. NIST 800-53 alignment: cis_benchmark_policies.nist_control_id column maps findings to NIST control families, but the plan does not specify which specific NIST control family mappings (AC-2, IA-5, SC-8, SI-2) are covered by the CIS Cisco IOS rules -- this is a compliance gap in the MVP design. GLBA Safeguards Rule requires annual risk assessment of networked systems -- this tool produces evidence for the network device component but the broader GLBA scope is not addressed.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Binary distribution: goreleaser produces GitHub Release with linux/amd64, linux/arm64, darwin/amd64, darwin/arm64 binaries and SHA-256 checksums.txt. IT staff download from GitHub Releases and install to /usr/local/bin. No package manager integration. Schema migrations run automatically on first launch via embedded golang-migrate scripts. GitHub Actions CI: go test ./... -race, go vet, staticcheck linting, govulncheck CVE scanning. Release trigger: git tag v1.x.y pushes to main triggers goreleaser. Note: CGO_ENABLED=1 required for SQLCipher means binaries cannot be fully static -- this conflicts with the 'single static binary' constraint in the contract and is not resolved in this plan.
target_output.observability: Structured JSON logging to stdout via slog package; --log-level flag (debug/info/warn/error). Scan progress bar via termenv library showing completed/total devices and current device hostname. Each scan_run record in SQLite captures operator_username, scan duration, device_count, and finding_count for local audit trail. No remote telemetry or monitoring -- tool is air-gapped from monitoring infrastructure by design. Report files named with scan_run_id and timestamp for manual archival by IT staff.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Zero hosting costs -- local binary. No external service dependencies. Build costs: GitHub Actions free tier sufficient for Go binary build and test. Distribution: GitHub Releases storage is free. Scaling: 20 concurrent SSH goroutines handles 200-device campus in under 10 minutes on standard IT workstation hardware. SQLite WAL mode handles concurrent report generation reads without blocking ongoing scan writes.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 -- Cisco IOS MVP (6 weeks): cobra CLI, SSH device connection, 50 CIS Cisco IOS v8 rules embedded as YAML, RuleEngine, SQLite+SQLCipher storage, HTML and JSON report generation, AES-256-GCM keystore, goreleaser binary release pipeline. Phase 2 -- Multi-Device and Compare (4 weeks): Palo Alto firewall rule set (30 rules), Aruba AP rule set (20 rules), SNMP device support, scan comparison command, nist_control_id column mapping to NIST 800-53 AC/IA/SC/SI families. Phase 3 -- Integration (3 weeks): SIEM Splunk export format, CI/CD scan scheduling via GitHub Actions cron, automated remediation report email distribution via sendmail.
target_output.development_guidelines: CIS rule definitions in YAML files under rules/ directory; embedded at build time via embed.FS. Rule check_expressions use a simple boolean expression language evaluated against config text -- no exec of shell commands. New device type support requires implementing the DeviceParser interface and adding YAML rule files. All SSH connections must use strict host key verification -- no test helpers that disable host key checking should be committed. govulncheck must pass in CI before binary release.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: SQLCipher CGO requirement prevents fully static binary builds -- goreleaser with CGO_ENABLED=1 produces dynamically linked binaries that require glibc on Linux targets; IT staff on musl-based systems (Alpine) will encounter runtime errors. This risk is not resolved in the current plan. Risk: CIS rule YAML files embedded in binary require recompilation for rule updates -- mitigated in Phase 2 by supporting external rule directory override via --rules-dir flag. Tradeoff: SQLite over PostgreSQL is appropriate for local single-operator storage but prevents multi-operator shared scan history without file synchronization. Risk: NIST 800-53 and GLBA compliance mapping is incomplete -- nist_control_id column is defined but specific control-to-rule mappings for GLBA Safeguards Rule network controls are not specified.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Max concurrent SSH connections set to 20 as default, configurable via --max-concurrent flag; tested against Cisco Catalyst 3650 which limits to 16 concurrent SSH sessions. YAML rule files chosen over HCL or JSON for human readability by IT staff updating rules. SQLCipher chosen over application-layer encryption for stronger database-level protection, accepting the CGO constraint.

metadata.notes: Intentionally flawed: plan references a web dashboard despite cli_tool class; CGO/single-static-binary conflict unresolved; NIST 800-53 and GLBA control mappings underspecified despite compliance_context contract requirement.

profile.domain: University Course Catalog REST API Migration

project_goal.value: Migrate the university's legacy SOAP-based course catalog service to a modern JSON REST API exposing searchable course listings, section schedules, and prerequisite chains for the student portal, mobile app, and third-party ed-tech partners
project_goal.rationale: Legacy SOAP service blocks modern client development and partner integrations requiring JSON APIs

target_users.value: Internal student portal and mobile app development teams and third-party ed-tech partner application developers
target_users.rationale: Three distinct consumer groups identified in IT and partnerships discovery sessions

project_class.rationale: Standalone API layer with no frontend; consumed programmatically by multiple client applications

capabilities.rationale: API service needs backend logic, relational data layer, OAuth2 auth, partner-facing API surface, and usage analytics

complexity_level.rationale: SOAP compatibility adapter pattern adds integration complexity; core course catalog domain is well-understood

risk_level.rationale: Course catalog data is non-sensitive internal academic information; no PII, financial, or health data

data_sensitivity.rationale: Course listings, section schedules, and prerequisites are internal academic data containing no personal information

external_exposure.rationale: Ed-tech partners require authenticated API access; not fully public but accessible beyond internal network

access_model.value: OAuth2 client credentials for partner applications; API key fallback for legacy integrations; internal consumers use service accounts with bearer tokens
access_model.rationale: Multi-tier access model supports modern OAuth2 partners and legacy API key consumers during 6-month parallel SOAP operation

feature_scope.value: Course search and filtering by subject, level, and keyword; section schedule retrieval by course and term; prerequisite chain traversal; subject area browsing; academic term catalog; SOAP compatibility adapter for legacy consumers; per-consumer API usage analytics
feature_scope.rationale: Full feature parity with legacy SOAP service required for complete consumer cutover

mvp_scope.value: Course search, section schedule retrieval, OAuth2 authentication, SOAP compatibility adapter for parallel operation during migration window
mvp_scope.rationale: MVP enables student portal cutover while legacy SOAP clients continue operating without disruption

security_baseline.value: OAuth2 client credentials with PKCE for partners, API key fallback with per-key rate limiting in Redis, TLS 1.3 in transit, no encryption at rest required for non-sensitive catalog data
security_baseline.rationale: Standard public API security controls appropriate for internal course catalog data

frontend_stack.value: none — API service only; no frontend in scope
frontend_stack.rationale: API service project class; consumed by existing client UIs maintained by separate teams

backend_stack.rationale: Go provides low latency for high-frequency catalog queries during registration periods; Chi is minimal with excellent middleware support

data_platform.value: PostgreSQL 16 for course catalog data; Redis 7 for response caching and rate limiting
data_platform.rationale: PostgreSQL supports complex prerequisite graph queries via recursive CTEs; Redis caches frequent schedule lookups

hosting_target.value: GCP Cloud Run with auto-scaling to zero in us-central1
hosting_target.rationale: Cloud Run matches catalog traffic patterns with zero idle cost between registration periods

privacy_retention_policy.value: API access logs retained 90 days; course catalog data retained for current plus 5 historical academic years for transcript verification
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Historical terms needed for transcript course name verification; 90-day log retention meets security audit requirements

future_scope.value: GraphQL endpoint for flexible client queries, real-time schedule change webhooks, integration with enrollment system for live seat availability
future_scope.rationale: Phase 2 enhancements planned after migration stabilization and partner feedback

constraints.value: Must operate alongside legacy SOAP service during 6-month parallel run; budget under $300/month; deploy to us-central1 for data residency alignment
constraints.rationale: Parallel operation ensures zero-disruption migration; budget constraint enforces serverless approach

observability_baseline.value: Google Cloud Monitoring for Cloud Run metrics, structured JSON logging with correlation IDs, per-consumer usage dashboards for partner SLA reporting
observability_baseline.rationale: GCP-native monitoring minimizes additional tooling cost; per-consumer dashboards enable partner SLA reporting

execution_preference.value: API-first development with OpenAPI 3.1 contract published before implementation; Kanban flow with weekly releases
execution_preference.rationale: Contract-first approach enables parallel development by client teams and API team during migration window

project.goal: Replace legacy SOAP course catalog with JSON REST API while operating in parallel for 6 months
project.target_users: Student portal, mobile app, ed-tech partners
project.timeline: 6-month migration window with parallel SOAP operation

backend.api: REST with OpenAPI 3.1; SOAP compatibility adapter at /soap/v1/catalog

security.auth: OAuth2 client credentials and API key fallback
security.transport: TLS 1.3 at Cloud Run load balancer

data.db: PostgreSQL 16 with full-text search tsvector index on course descriptions
data.cache: Redis 7 for schedule and prerequisite response caching

devops.ci: Cloud Build with go test and staticcheck gates

reasoner_reviews.product: Course catalog migration is well-scoped for MVP. The SOAP adapter enables safe parallel operation during the 6-month transition without disrupting legacy consumers. Prerequisite chain traversal via recursive CTE is a critical feature for the student portal course planning feature — must be included in MVP, not deferred.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Go with Chi is a strong fit for a high-read, low-write catalog API. SOAP adapter should be a thin translation layer sharing the same PostgreSQL data layer as the REST handlers — not calling the legacy SOAP service — to avoid dual source-of-truth during parallel operation. Redis caching of section schedules is essential for registration period traffic when the same schedule data is queried thousands of times per hour.
reasoner_reviews.security: OAuth2 client credentials are appropriate for server-to-server partner access. API key fallback must maintain independent rate limit buckets in Redis to prevent key abuse. The SOAP compatibility adapter must enforce the same auth middleware as the REST endpoints — no unauthenticated path through the adapter.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Cloud Run auto-scaling to zero keeps costs minimal outside registration periods. The $300/month budget is achievable with Cloud SQL PostgreSQL and Memorystore Redis at small instance tiers. The 6-month parallel SOAP operation requires the adapter to share the same data source as REST handlers to ensure consistency.
reasoner_reviews.critic: The OpenAPI 3.1 specification must be published and frozen before any consumer team begins integration — breaking changes after publication will delay the student portal cutover. Define a versioning strategy and deprecation header policy for the SOAP adapter from day one.

backend.service_design: Go Chi API with route groups: /v1/courses (search, lookup), /v1/sections (schedule by course and term), /v1/prerequisites (graph traversal), /v1/terms (academic term catalog), /v1/subjects (subject area browsing). SOAP adapter as separate handler group /soap/v1/catalog sharing the same service layer and PostgreSQL data source.
backend.api_patterns: GET /v1/courses?q=STRING&subject=CODE&term=TERM_CODE&level=ENUM returning paginated JSON. GET /v1/courses/:course_code/prerequisites returning recursive prerequisite graph up to 5 levels. GET /v1/sections?course_code=STRING&term=TERM_CODE for schedule data. POST /v1/oauth/token for OAuth2 client credentials flow. All responses: application/json.
backend.failure_handling: Redis circuit breaker for cache reads with fallback to PostgreSQL. Exponential backoff on Cloud SQL connection retries with jitter. SOAP adapter returns valid SOAP faults on downstream errors preserving protocol compatibility for legacy consumers.
backend.scaling_notes: Cloud Run min-instances=0, max-instances=10. CPU-based scaling at 60% utilization. Section schedule endpoint caches at Redis with 15-minute TTL; cache warm Cloud Scheduler job runs after SIS publishes updated schedule data.

security.auth_design: OAuth2 client credentials at POST /v1/oauth/token returning HMAC-SHA256 signed JWTs with 1-hour expiry. API key in X-API-Key header validated against bcrypt hash in PostgreSQL with Redis lookup cache. SOAP adapter validates API key in WS-Security UsernameToken header.
security.authorization_model: Consumer tiers: internal (full catalog access), partner (course and schedule read, no administrative fields), preview (limited field set). Scopes encoded in JWT claims.
security.abuse_prevention: Redis token bucket per consumer: partner tier 1000 req/hour, internal 10000 req/hour. Burst allowance 2x sustained rate for registration period spikes. 429 response with Retry-After header.

data.entities: courses (id UUID PK, course_code VARCHAR(12) UNIQUE, title TEXT, description TEXT, description_ts tsvector GENERATED, subject_area_id FK, credits NUMERIC(3,1), level ENUM undergrad/grad/professional, is_active BOOLEAN). course_sections (id UUID PK, course_id FK, term_id FK, instructor_id FK, crn VARCHAR(10), meeting_days VARCHAR(20), start_time TIME, end_time TIME, location VARCHAR(100), enrolled_count INT, max_capacity INT, status ENUM open/closed/cancelled). prerequisites (id UUID PK, course_id FK, required_course_id FK, prerequisite_type ENUM required/recommended/concurrent, minimum_grade CHAR(2)). academic_terms (id UUID PK, term_code VARCHAR(10) UNIQUE, name, start_date DATE, end_date DATE, registration_open_at TIMESTAMPTZ). api_consumers (id UUID PK, name, tier ENUM internal/partner/preview, oauth_client_id VARCHAR(64), api_key_hash VARCHAR(128), rate_limit_per_hour INT).
data.storage_design: PostgreSQL 16 on Cloud SQL with tsvector GIN index on courses for full-text search. Recursive CTE for prerequisite graph traversal with cycle detection clause. Redis Memorystore for section schedule and prerequisite response caching with 15-minute TTL.

devops.deployment_topology: Cloud Run service in us-central1 pulling container from Artifact Registry. Cloud SQL PostgreSQL in same region. Memorystore Redis in same VPC via Private Service Connect. Cloud Scheduler triggers cache warm job nightly after SIS schedule publish.
devops.ci_cd_design: Cloud Build pipeline: go vet, go test -race, staticcheck, docker build, push to Artifact Registry, deploy to Cloud Run staging, API smoke test suite, promote to production with 10% traffic split for 30 minutes. OpenAPI spec validation gate before every deploy.
devops.observability_stack: Google Cloud Monitoring: Cloud Run request count, latency p50/p95/p99 per endpoint. Structured JSON logs with consumer_id and correlation_id. Custom per-consumer dashboard for API usage volume and error rates. BigQuery export for partner SLA reporting.

target_output.thinking_summary: Designing a SOAP-to-REST migration API for the university course catalog. Core architectural decision: the SOAP compatibility adapter shares the same Go service layer and PostgreSQL data source as the REST handlers — no calls to the legacy SOAP service — ensuring data consistency during parallel operation. Chi route groups cleanly separate REST and SOAP surfaces. Redis caching of section schedules is critical for registration period traffic spikes. Cloud Run auto-scaling to zero keeps the sub-$300/month budget achievable.
target_output.title: University Course Catalog REST API Migration
target_output.executive_summary: A Go-based REST API service on GCP Cloud Run migrating the university's legacy SOAP course catalog to a modern JSON API, with a SOAP compatibility adapter enabling 6 months of parallel operation. Exposes searchable course listings, section schedules, and prerequisite chains to the student portal, mobile app, and ed-tech partners via OAuth2 and API key authentication. PostgreSQL 16 with full-text search and Redis caching handle catalog queries at registration-period scale within a $300/month budget.
target_output.architecture_overview: Single Go service on Cloud Run with two handler groups sharing a common service layer: REST handlers under /v1/* serving JSON responses, and a SOAP compatibility adapter under /soap/v1/* translating legacy SOAP envelopes to internal service calls against the same PostgreSQL 16 data source. Redis Memorystore caches section schedule responses with 15-minute TTL refreshed by Cloud Scheduler. OAuth2 client credentials with HMAC-SHA256 JWT signing handles partner authentication; API key header validation handles legacy consumers. Cloud SQL PostgreSQL uses recursive CTEs for prerequisite graph traversal and tsvector GIN indexes for course full-text search.
target_output.technology_stack: Backend: Go 1.22 with Chi v5 router, encoding/xml for SOAP adapter, net/http stdlib. Data: PostgreSQL 16 on Cloud SQL with tsvector and GIN index for course search, Redis 7 on Memorystore for response caching and rate limiting. Auth: custom OAuth2 server with HMAC-SHA256 JWT signing; GCP Secret Manager for client secrets and API key salts. Hosting: GCP Cloud Run us-central1 with Artifact Registry. CI/CD: Cloud Build with staticcheck and go test -race. Observability: Google Cloud Monitoring, structured JSON logs, BigQuery for usage analytics.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Course Search: full-text search across course title and description with subject area, academic level, and term filters returning paginated JSON. Section Schedule Retrieval: query open sections by course code and term with meeting pattern, location, and enrollment capacity. Prerequisite Graph: GET /v1/courses/:course_code/prerequisites returns multi-level chain via recursive CTE with cycle detection, showing required, recommended, and concurrent prerequisites with minimum grade thresholds. Academic Term Catalog: list past and future terms with registration window dates. SOAP Adapter: translate legacy operations (GetCourseList, GetSectionSchedule) to internal service calls returning SOAP envelopes for legacy consumers. Consumer Management: OAuth2 client registration and API key issuance with tier-based scope assignment.
target_output.system_components: Cloud Run service container (Go binary, multi-stage Docker build, approximately 18 MB image). Cloud SQL PostgreSQL 16 (db-f1-micro for dev, db-g1-small for production with 30 GB SSD). Memorystore Redis 7 (1 GB basic tier in same VPC). Cloud Scheduler job for nightly cache warm after SIS schedule publish and daily analytics export. Artifact Registry repository for container images. Secret Manager for OAuth2 signing keys and API key bcrypt salts. BigQuery dataset receiving Cloud Logging export for per-consumer usage analytics.
target_output.workflows: Course Search Workflow: Partner application sends GET /v1/courses?q=introduction+to+statistics&subject=MATH&term=2026-spring with OAuth2 bearer token. Chi middleware validates JWT, extracts consumer tier and scopes. Handler queries PostgreSQL tsvector index filtered by subject and term, returns paginated JSON. Redis cache hit on repeat queries returns in under 5ms. Prerequisite Chain Traversal: Client sends GET /v1/courses/CS301/prerequisites. Service executes recursive CTE traversing the prerequisites table up to 5 levels with cycle detection, returns graph with prerequisite_type (required/recommended/concurrent) and minimum_grade edges. SOAP Compatibility Workflow: Legacy integration sends SOAP envelope to /soap/v1/catalog. Adapter validates WS-Security UsernameToken against API key hash, parses SOAP body to extract operation name and parameters, calls identical Go service layer as REST handlers, serializes response into SOAP envelope preserving legacy field names. Both surfaces share the same PostgreSQL data source ensuring consistency.
target_output.data_model: courses (id UUID PK, course_code VARCHAR(12) UNIQUE, title TEXT NOT NULL, description TEXT, description_ts tsvector GENERATED ALWAYS AS (to_tsvector('english', coalesce(title,'') || ' ' || coalesce(description,''))) STORED, subject_area_id FK, credits NUMERIC(3,1), level ENUM undergrad/grad/professional, is_active BOOLEAN DEFAULT TRUE, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ, updated_at TIMESTAMPTZ). course_sections (id UUID PK, course_id FK, term_id FK, instructor_id FK NULLABLE, crn VARCHAR(10), meeting_days VARCHAR(20), start_time TIME, end_time TIME, location VARCHAR(100), enrolled_count INT DEFAULT 0, max_capacity INT, status ENUM open/closed/cancelled, waitlist_count INT DEFAULT 0). prerequisites (id UUID PK, course_id FK, required_course_id FK, prerequisite_type ENUM required/recommended/concurrent, minimum_grade CHAR(2)). academic_terms (id UUID PK, term_code VARCHAR(10) UNIQUE, name TEXT, start_date DATE, end_date DATE, registration_open_at TIMESTAMPTZ, registration_close_at TIMESTAMPTZ). subject_areas (id UUID PK, code VARCHAR(8) UNIQUE, name TEXT, college TEXT). api_consumers (id UUID PK, name TEXT, tier ENUM internal/partner/preview, oauth_client_id VARCHAR(64), api_key_hash VARCHAR(128), rate_limit_per_hour INT, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ).
target_output.api_design: OpenAPI 3.1 spec at /openapi.json. GET /v1/courses?q=STRING&subject=CODE&term=TERM_CODE&level=ENUM&page=INT&limit=INT returns {courses: [...], total, page, limit}. GET /v1/courses/:course_code returns single course detail. GET /v1/courses/:course_code/prerequisites returns {prerequisites: [{course_code, prerequisite_type, minimum_grade, level}], depth}. GET /v1/sections?course_code=STRING&term=TERM_CODE&status=open returns sections with enrollment counts. GET /v1/terms returns academic terms with registration windows. POST /v1/oauth/token (client_credentials grant) returns JWT. POST /soap/v1/catalog handles SOAP envelope for legacy consumers. All error responses: {error: STRING, code: STRING, request_id: STRING}.
target_output.security_and_compliance: OAuth2 client credentials: POST /v1/oauth/token returns HMAC-SHA256 JWT with 1-hour expiry; client secrets in Secret Manager. API key: X-API-Key header validated against bcrypt hash in PostgreSQL with 5-minute Redis lookup cache; plaintext key returned only at issuance. Consumer tier enforcement: partner-tier consumers cannot access internal administrative fields (sis_code, internal_notes). Rate limiting: Redis token bucket per consumer ID, 1000 req/hour for partners, 10000 for internal; 429 with Retry-After header. TLS 1.3 at Cloud Run load balancer. SOAP adapter validates WS-Security UsernameToken through the same auth middleware as REST handlers — no unauthenticated path through the adapter.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Cloud Run service us-central1: min-instances=0, max-instances=10, CPU limit=1, memory=512Mi, concurrency=100. Cloud SQL db-g1-small with 30 GB SSD, automated daily backups to GCS with 7-day retention. Memorystore Redis 1 GB basic tier in same VPC via Private Service Connect. Cloud Build pipeline: go vet, staticcheck, go test -race, docker build, push to Artifact Registry, deploy staging Cloud Run revision, API smoke test suite against /v1/courses and /v1/terms, 10% traffic split to new revision for 30 minutes, promote to 100%. Rollback: revert traffic split to previous Cloud Run revision. Separate Cloud SQL instances per dev/staging/production environment.
target_output.observability: Google Cloud Monitoring dashboards: Cloud Run request rate and latency p50/p95/p99 per endpoint, 4xx and 5xx error rates per consumer tier. Structured JSON logs: consumer_id, endpoint, method, status_code, duration_ms, correlation_id, cache_hit BOOLEAN. Cloud Logging export to BigQuery for per-consumer request volume and error rate analytics. Alert policies: error rate >2% for 5-minute window, p99 latency >500ms for /v1/courses search, Cloud SQL connections >80%.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Projected $180-240/month at steady state: Cloud Run CPU-on-request only approximately $40, Cloud SQL db-g1-small approximately $70, Memorystore Redis 1 GB approximately $50, Cloud Build approximately $20, egress approximately $20. Registration period spike: Cloud Run scales to 10 instances automatically; Redis absorbs repeat schedule queries reducing Cloud SQL load by approximately 70% during peak booking. Cost ceiling enforced by Cloud Run max-instances=10 cap keeping monthly cost predictable.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (6 weeks): PostgreSQL schema, course search and section schedule REST endpoints, OAuth2 client credentials auth, API key auth, SOAP adapter for GetCourseList and GetSectionSchedule operations, OpenAPI 3.1 spec published to Stoplight, Cloud Run deployment pipeline. Phase 2 (3 weeks): prerequisite graph traversal endpoint, academic term management, Redis caching for section schedules and prerequisites, per-consumer BigQuery usage analytics. Phase 3 (3 weeks): partner onboarding documentation portal, SOAP adapter deprecation headers with migration timeline, load testing for registration period traffic, cache warm Cloud Scheduler job.
target_output.development_guidelines: OpenAPI 3.1 spec is the source of truth — no endpoint ships without a spec entry and spec changes require partner team review. Go handler tests use httptest.NewRecorder for every endpoint. PostgreSQL integration tests use testcontainers-go with fixture course data including multi-level prerequisite chains. SOAP adapter tested with recorded legacy request/response pairs as golden test files. staticcheck and go vet in Cloud Build gate; no suppressed linter warnings without documented justification. Recursive CTE for prerequisite traversal tested with cycle detection fixture to verify no infinite loops.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: SOAP adapter falling out of sync if the data model evolves during the 6-month parallel run. Mitigation: adapter and REST handlers call the same Go service layer — schema changes are reflected in both surfaces simultaneously with no adapter-specific code paths. Risk: prerequisite cycle in legacy course data. Mitigation: recursive CTE uses WITH CYCLE clause; detected cycles logged as data quality alerts and excluded from response with an explanation field. Tradeoff: Cloud Run to-zero causes 200-400ms cold start latency. Mitigation: min-instances=1 for production during October and February registration windows at approximately $30 additional monthly cost. Tradeoff: single Cloud Run service for REST and SOAP coupling deployment units. Mitigation: separate route groups with independent integration test suites make divergence visible in CI.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Selected Cloud Run over GKE for serverless zero-idle-cost scaling given low off-peak traffic volume. Chose PostgreSQL tsvector over Elasticsearch for course search given moderate query volume and straightforward query patterns. Decided SOAP adapter shares the same Go service layer as REST handlers rather than proxying to the legacy SOAP service to avoid dual source-of-truth risk. OAuth2 client credentials chosen for partners to support future scope-based access control without credential rotation.

metadata.notes: SOAP-to-REST course catalog migration on GCP Cloud Run with SOAP adapter for 6-month parallel operation and Redis caching for registration period scale.

project_goal.value: CLI tool for K-12 district administrators to migrate student and staff user accounts from Google Workspace for Education to Microsoft 365 Education, handling bulk account creation, group membership mapping, calendar migration, and email forwarding setup during the transition period
project_goal.rationale: Manual migration via admin consoles is error-prone at district scale of 15,000 accounts

target_users.value: District IT administrators running migrations on district workstations
target_users.rationale: Single operator persona identified in IT department discovery

project_class.rationale: No browser UI needed; IT admins operate from terminal with scripted batch workflows

capabilities.rationale: CLI needs batch processing logic, SQLite state tracking, Google and Microsoft API integrations

complexity_level.rationale: Two major API integration surfaces with batch processing and rollback state management

risk_level.rationale: Handles student personal data during migration; account mapping errors could disrupt student access

data_sensitivity.rationale: Student names, email addresses, group memberships, and calendar events are personal data

external_exposure.rationale: CLI runs only on district IT workstations with no external network exposure

access_model.value: OAuth2 authorization code flow for Google Workspace Admin SDK and Microsoft Graph API; admin credentials stored in OS keychain, never in config files
access_model.rationale: OAuth2 delegates authentication to identity providers; no district admin passwords stored in plaintext

feature_scope.value: Bulk account creation in Microsoft 365, Google Group to Microsoft 365 Group mapping, calendar event migration, email forwarding setup, dry-run mode, rollback checkpoints, error log reporting
feature_scope.rationale: Full migration feature set covering accounts, groups, calendar, and mail continuity

mvp_scope.value: User account creation in Microsoft 365, group membership mapping, email forwarding setup, dry-run simulation, migration batch state tracking in SQLite
mvp_scope.rationale: Account and mail continuity are highest priority for school-year cutover

security_baseline.value: OAuth2 tokens stored in OS keychain via keyring library, never in files or environment variables; SQLite migration state file permissions restricted to owning user; no student data written to disk beyond SQLite migration log
security_baseline.rationale: Credential and data protection controls appropriate for IT workstation tool handling student records

frontend_stack.value: none — CLI only, stdout/stderr output, rich terminal formatting via Python rich library
frontend_stack.rationale: CLI tool project class; no browser interface

backend_stack.rationale: Click provides composable command groups; Python has mature Google and Microsoft API SDKs

data_platform.value: SQLite for migration batch state and error logs — local, no external database
data_platform.rationale: Self-contained SQLite requires no infrastructure; stores migration progress, conflict records, and rollback checkpoints

hosting_target.value: District IT workstations running Python 3.12; distributed as a pip-installable package via PyPI
hosting_target.rationale: IT admins run the tool locally; no cloud hosting needed

privacy_retention_policy.value: SQLite migration state files must be deleted by IT admin after migration completion and audit sign-off; no migration logs retained beyond 30 days post-migration
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: FERPA requires minimizing retention of student personal data records; migration artifacts are temporary

future_scope.value: SharePoint file migration, Teams channel provisioning from Google Classroom, automated post-migration validation report
future_scope.rationale: Phase 2 migration scope covers collaboration tools after accounts and mail are stable

constraints.value: Must run on Python 3.12 on Windows and macOS workstations; no dependency on Docker; API rate limits: Google Admin SDK 2400 req/min, Microsoft Graph 10000 req/10min
constraints.rationale: District IT workstations are Windows-majority; Docker not installed on admin machines

observability_baseline.value: Structured migration progress output to stdout with Rich progress bars; detailed error log in SQLite with stack traces; summary report printed to stdout at batch completion
observability_baseline.rationale: IT admins need real-time visibility into migration batch progress and errors from the terminal

execution_preference.value: Iterative development with internal IT admin beta testing after each major command group is complete
execution_preference.rationale: IT admin feedback on UX and error handling is critical for a tool that runs on real student data

compliance_context.value: FERPA — student account data and email contents are educational records; data portability must preserve access for legitimate educational purposes; migration log must document chain of custody
compliance_context.rationale: Student account records are educational records; FERPA governs access and retention

project.goal: Automate bulk migration of 15,000 Google Workspace accounts to Microsoft 365 with state tracking and rollback
project.target_users: District IT administrators
project.timeline: 90-day migration window before school year start

backend.framework: Click CLI framework with Rich for terminal output
backend.api: Google Admin SDK + Google Calendar API; Microsoft Graph API

security.auth: OAuth2 via Google and Microsoft identity providers, tokens in OS keychain
security.data_handling: No student data written to disk except SQLite migration state; state file restricted to 0o600 permissions

data.db: SQLite 3 for migration batch state, error logs, rollback checkpoints
data.state_tracking: Migration batches keyed by batch_id with per-user status enum

devops.ci: GitHub Actions for test suite and pip package publish
devops.distribution: PyPI package install via pip or pipx
devops.environments: IT admin workstation — Windows 10+ and macOS 13+

constraints.platform: Windows and macOS, no Docker requirement

reasoner_reviews.product: Account and mail continuity are correctly prioritized for MVP. Dry-run mode is essential and must be implemented before any live migration commands — IT admins need to validate the mapping before committing changes at 15,000-account scale. The group membership mapping from Google Groups to Microsoft 365 Groups is the most error-prone step and needs robust conflict detection with a YAML override file for manual resolution.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Click command groups are the right structure: top-level commands for auth, migrate users, migrate groups, migrate calendar, and report. SQLite state tracking enables resumable batches if the tool crashes mid-migration, which is critical for large runs. Microsoft Graph JSON batching at 20 requests per batch call is essential to stay within rate limits. The Google and Microsoft SDK clients should be instantiated once per command invocation via Click context to avoid redundant token refreshes.
reasoner_reviews.security: OAuth2 tokens in the OS keychain via keyring library is the correct approach — never environment variables or config files. The SQLite state file stores FERPA-protected account mapping data; file permissions must be restricted to 0o600 on Linux/macOS and ACL-restricted on Windows via icacls. The dry-run flag must be enforced at the API call layer, not just at the UI presentation level, to prevent accidental writes.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: API rate limiting is a real constraint at 15,000 accounts. Exponential backoff with jitter is required for both Google and Microsoft API calls. Microsoft Graph batch endpoint reduces call volume by 20x for account creation. Batch size of 500 users per CLI invocation allows progress checkpointing without overwhelming SQLite write throughput.

backend.service_design: Click application with command groups: auth (login-google, login-microsoft, logout, status), migrate (users, groups, calendar, forwarding), report (summary, errors, diff), rollback (users, groups). Each command module imports shared API client factories and the SQLite state manager via Click pass_context decorator.
backend.api_patterns: Google Admin SDK DirectoryService for user and group reads. Microsoft Graph SDK for Python for user creation via POST /users batched in JSON batch envelopes. Calendar migration via Google Calendar API list/insert and Microsoft Graph calendar events POST. Batch API calls using Microsoft Graph JSON batching with 20 requests per envelope to maximize throughput within rate limits.
backend.failure_handling: Exponential backoff with jitter decorator applied to all Google and Microsoft API calls. Per-user retry queue in SQLite failed_accounts table with error_code, attempt_count, last_attempt_at columns. Migration batch not marked complete until all retries exhausted or admin manually marks records as skipped. SIGINT handler flushes SQLite WAL before exit.

security.auth_design: keyring library stores Google OAuth2 refresh token under service name 'district-migrator-google' and Microsoft OAuth2 refresh token under 'district-migrator-microsoft'. Tokens refreshed automatically by SDK clients. auth status command validates token expiry without printing token values to stdout.
security.secrets_management: No credentials in config files, environment variables, or command-line arguments. OAuth2 client_id for registered app stored in package as non-sensitive public value — delegated user auth only. SQLite database file created with os.chmod 0o600 on all POSIX platforms; Windows uses icacls to restrict to current user.
security.privacy_controls: Student data in SQLite limited to user_id, UPN, display_name, migration_status, error_message. No email body content, calendar event details, or group content stored in SQLite. Summary report outputs only aggregate counts and error codes, not individual student identifiers.

data.entities: migration_batches (id INTEGER PK AUTOINCREMENT, batch_name TEXT, operation ENUM users/groups/calendar/forwarding, started_at TEXT, completed_at TEXT, total INT, succeeded INT, failed INT). user_accounts (id INTEGER PK, batch_id FK, google_user_id TEXT, google_email TEXT NOT NULL, ms365_upn TEXT, display_name TEXT, status TEXT ENUM pending/succeeded/failed/skipped, error_message TEXT, ms365_object_id TEXT, attempts INT DEFAULT 0). group_mappings (id INTEGER PK, batch_id FK, google_group_email TEXT, ms365_group_id TEXT, member_count INT, status TEXT, manual_override INT DEFAULT 0). rollback_checkpoints (id INTEGER PK, batch_id FK, checkpoint_name TEXT UNIQUE, state_snapshot TEXT, created_at TEXT). error_logs (id INTEGER PK, user_id INT FK, operation TEXT, http_status INT, api_error_code TEXT, stack_trace TEXT, recorded_at TEXT).
data.schema_notes: SQLite WAL mode enabled for crash-safe writes during long-running batches. user_accounts indexed on batch_id and status for efficient pending/failed querying during resume. rollback_checkpoints stores JSON snapshot of batch state before each major operation phase enabling selective account deletion on rollback.

devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions matrix: Python 3.12 on ubuntu-latest and windows-latest runners. Steps: pip install -e .[dev], pytest --cov=district_migrator --cov-fail-under=80, mypy --strict district_migrator, ruff check. Release workflow: tag-triggered, python -m build wheel, twine upload to PyPI via trusted publishing OIDC token.
devops.deployment_topology: pip install district-migrator or pipx install district-migrator registers CLI entry point in PATH. pyproject.toml declares dependencies: Click>=8.1, rich>=13.0, google-api-python-client>=2.120, msgraph-sdk>=1.5, keyring>=25.0, msal>=1.28. Supports Python 3.12 on Windows 10+, macOS 13+, Ubuntu 22+.

target_output.thinking_summary: Designing a CLI migration tool for Google Workspace to Microsoft 365 at K-12 district scale of 15,000 accounts.
