target_output.thinking_summary: Revising HIPAA-compliant campus health mobile app to fix critical PHI storage violation and FHIR resilience gap from round 1. ISS-001 fix: replace all AsyncStorage with Expo SecureStore which uses iOS Keychain and Android Keystore, ensuring no PHI ever touches unencrypted device storage. ISS-002 fix: add circuit breaker to FHIR adapter with Redis-cached provider availability as fallback, isolating Epic maintenance windows from core booking functionality. Also fixing audit_log schema to include device_id per HIPAA device-level access tracking requirement.

fix_report.action_taken: Replaced all React Native AsyncStorage usage with Expo SecureStore. Session tokens, Cognito refresh tokens, and any patient name display cache now stored in Expo SecureStore which maps to iOS Keychain and Android Keystore. Added ESLint rule forbidding AsyncStorage imports in the mobile codebase.
fix_report.expected_outcome: No PHI or session credentials stored in unencrypted device storage; HIPAA mobile device encryption safeguard satisfied.

fix_report.action_taken: Added circuit breaker (opossum library) to the FHIR adapter module with 30-second open window on 3 consecutive failures. Provider availability slots cached in Redis with 15-minute TTL. During FHIR circuit open state, booking flow uses cached availability from Redis and displays a notice that real-time availability may be delayed; visit summaries show a degraded-mode placeholder.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Epic FHIR maintenance windows no longer cause complete appointment booking failure; app degrades gracefully to cached availability data.

target_output.title: Campus Health Services Mobile App — HIPAA-Compliant Architecture
target_output.executive_summary: A HIPAA-compliant React Native mobile app for university campus health services enabling 12,000 enrolled students to book appointments, complete pre-visit intake forms, view visit summaries from Epic EHR via FHIR R4, and receive push notifications for appointment reminders. Fastify backend on AWS ECS Fargate with PostgreSQL and KMS encryption handles PHI with Cognito MFA auth. Round 2 revisions address critical PHI storage violation by replacing AsyncStorage with Expo SecureStore, and add an Epic FHIR circuit breaker with Redis-cached availability fallback.
target_output.architecture_overview: Three-layer architecture: React Native mobile client communicating over TLS 1.3 to a Fastify REST API on ECS Fargate, with a WebSocket endpoint for real-time secure messaging. Backend connects to RDS PostgreSQL with KMS-encrypted PHI fields and ElastiCache Redis for provider availability caching. An isolated FHIR adapter module with an opossum circuit breaker calls the Epic FHIR R4 API for visit summaries and immunization records, falling back to cached data during Epic outages. Cognito user pool with HIPAA-eligible configuration enforces MFA for all users. All PHI access events logged to an append-only audit_log table forwarded to CloudWatch Logs with 6-year retention.
target_output.technology_stack: Mobile: React Native 0.74, TypeScript, Expo SDK 51, Expo SecureStore for credential and sensitive data storage, Expo Notifications for push. State: React Query for server state, Zustand for local UI state. Backend: Node.js 20, Fastify with Zod schema validation, opossum circuit breaker for FHIR adapter. Data: RDS PostgreSQL 16 Multi-AZ with KMS, AWS Encryption SDK for application-layer PHI encryption, ElastiCache Redis 7. Auth: AWS Cognito HIPAA-eligible with MFA, SAML federation for university SSO. Infra: ECS Fargate us-east-1, ALB with WAF, S3 KMS for intake document attachments. Observability: Datadog APM with PHI masking, CloudWatch, PagerDuty.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Appointment Booking: student selects provider and appointment type, views available slots from Redis-cached availability refreshed from Epic FHIR every 15 minutes, confirms booking which writes to PostgreSQL appointments table and sends push confirmation via Expo Notifications. Pre-Visit Intake: student completes a JSON-template-rendered intake form before appointment; responses encrypted with AWS Encryption SDK and stored in intake_submissions. Visit Summary: read-only view of provider notes from Epic FHIR Encounter resources, served through FHIR adapter. Provider Queue: providers view their daily patient appointments, open intake form submissions, and mark appointments complete. Secure Messaging: real-time provider-patient messaging via WebSocket with messages stored encrypted in PostgreSQL. Push Reminders: appointment reminder notifications 24 hours and 1 hour before scheduled time via Expo Notifications APNs/FCM.
target_output.system_components: React Native mobile app (iOS and Android) with Expo SecureStore for token storage and FLAG_SECURE for PHI screen screenshot prevention. ECS Fargate cluster: API service (2-4 tasks auto-scaling CPU 70%), background worker for push notification dispatch and FHIR sync. ALB with WAF rules filtering OWASP Top 10 injection patterns. RDS PostgreSQL 16 Multi-AZ with KMS-encrypted storage and application-layer AWS Encryption SDK for PHI columns. ElastiCache Redis 7 for provider availability slots and WebSocket session state — no PHI in Redis. FHIR adapter module with opossum circuit breaker caching Epic API responses in Redis. Cognito user pool (HIPAA-eligible) with MFA and SAML. S3 bucket with KMS for intake form document attachments. CloudWatch Logs with 6-year log retention for HIPAA audit trail.
target_output.workflows: Appointment Booking Workflow: Student opens booking flow, selects provider Dr. Chen (Internal Medicine). App requests GET /v1/providers/chen/availability which checks Redis cache first; if cache hit returns slots from Redis, if miss calls Epic FHIR Slot resources via FHIR adapter and caches response for 15 minutes. Student selects Thursday 2pm slot, app POSTs /v1/appointments with appointment_type=wellness, provider_id, scheduled_at. Fastify handler writes to PostgreSQL appointments table and enqueues push notification for 24h and 1h reminders. If FHIR circuit breaker is open, cached availability is served with X-Availability-Source: cached header and a UI banner. Intake Submission Workflow: Night before appointment, student receives push notification linking to intake form. Student opens intake form rendered from JSON template for wellness visit (chief_complaint, current_medications, allergies, recent_symptoms). On submit, responses serialized to JSON, encrypted with AWS Encryption SDK, stored in intake_submissions with appointment_id FK. Provider sees intake flag on their appointment queue. Audit event written to audit_log: user_id=student, resource_type=intake_form, resource_id=submission_id, action=create, device_id=expo_device_id.
target_output.data_model: patients (id UUID PK, cognito_sub UNIQUE, student_id VARCHAR(20), first_name_enc BYTEA, last_name_enc BYTEA, dob_enc BYTEA, email_enc BYTEA, phone_enc BYTEA, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ). appointments (id UUID PK, patient_id FK, provider_id FK, appointment_type ENUM wellness/sick_visit/mental_health/immunization, scheduled_at TIMESTAMPTZ, duration_minutes INT, status ENUM scheduled/completed/cancelled/no_show, cancellation_reason TEXT, fhir_encounter_id VARCHAR(64), idempotency_key VARCHAR(64) UNIQUE). intake_submissions (id UUID PK, appointment_id FK, form_template_id FK, responses_enc BYTEA, submitted_at TIMESTAMPTZ). providers (id UUID PK, cognito_sub UNIQUE, name TEXT, specialty TEXT, fhir_practitioner_id VARCHAR(64)). messages (id UUID PK, sender_id FK, recipient_id FK, appointment_id FK, content_enc BYTEA, sent_at TIMESTAMPTZ, read_at TIMESTAMPTZ). audit_logs (id UUID PK, user_id UUID, resource_type TEXT, resource_id UUID, action TEXT, ip_address INET, device_id TEXT, user_agent TEXT, timestamp TIMESTAMPTZ) — append-only via trigger preventing UPDATE and DELETE.
target_output.api_design: All endpoints require Cognito JWT Bearer token; provider-scoped endpoints require provider role claim. POST /v1/appointments — book appointment with idempotency_key header. GET /v1/appointments?patient_id=UUID — list patient appointments. PATCH /v1/appointments/:id/cancel — cancel with cancellation_reason. GET /v1/providers/:id/availability?date=YYYY-MM-DD — availability slots (Redis-cached, FHIR-sourced). POST /v1/intake/:appointment_id — submit encrypted intake form. GET /v1/visits/:fhir_encounter_id/summary — visit summary from Epic FHIR via adapter. GET /v1/patients/:id/profile — patient demographics (PHI, patient-only scope). WebSocket /ws/messages — real-time secure messaging. All error responses: {error: STRING, code: STRING, request_id: STRING}. Rate limiting: 100 req/min per user via Redis token bucket.
target_output.security_and_compliance: HIPAA technical safeguards implemented: (1) Encryption at rest — RDS KMS encryption plus AWS Encryption SDK for PHI column-level encryption; S3 server-side KMS for intake documents. (2) Encryption in transit — TLS 1.3 at ALB; WebSocket connections over WSS. (3) Access controls — Cognito MFA required for all accounts; RBAC patient/provider; providers can only access records of patients with scheduled or completed appointments. (4) Audit controls — append-only audit_log with trigger preventing modification; logs forwarded to CloudWatch Logs 6-year retention; device_id from Expo Constants captured for every PHI access event. (5) Automatic logoff — Cognito session tokens expire after 15 minutes of inactivity; biometric re-auth for continued access. (6) Mobile device safeguards — Expo SecureStore for all credential and sensitive data storage (iOS Keychain, Android Keystore); FLAG_SECURE on Android PHI screens prevents screenshots; no PHI in AsyncStorage. BAA with AWS for all HIPAA-eligible services.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: ECS Fargate cluster us-east-1: API service 2-4 tasks (auto-scale CPU 70%), background worker 1-2 tasks for push dispatch and FHIR sync. RDS PostgreSQL 16 Multi-AZ in private subnet, 100 GB SSD, automated daily backups 35-day retention to encrypted S3. ElastiCache Redis 7 single-node with in-transit encryption in same VPC. ALB with WAF (OWASP Core Rule Set v3.2) in public subnet. Secrets in AWS Secrets Manager: DB credentials, Epic FHIR client credentials, Expo push token, Datadog API key. CI/CD: GitHub Actions pipeline — jest unit tests, OWASP Dependency Check CVE scan, Docker build and ECR push, ECS staging deploy, API integration test suite with mocked Epic FHIR responses, manual security review gate for PHI-handling code changes, production rolling deploy. Rollback: revert ECS task definition to prior revision.
target_output.observability: Datadog APM on ECS Fargate sidecar: trace all API requests with PHI field masking rules blocking first_name, last_name, dob, diagnosis fields from appearing in APM traces or logs. CloudWatch Logs: structured JSON with correlation_id, user_id (hashed), endpoint, status_code, duration_ms, fhir_circuit_state. Custom Datadog dashboards: appointment booking rate per hour, intake submission rate, Epic FHIR p95 latency, circuit breaker state (open/closed), push notification delivery rate. PagerDuty alerts: API error rate >1% for 5 minutes, p99 latency >3s, RDS connections >80%, PHI access anomaly (>50 unique patients accessed by single provider in 1 hour), FHIR circuit breaker open >10 minutes.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Projected $1600-1900/month at 12,000 enrolled students with 800 daily appointments: ECS Fargate 3 tasks average ~$200, RDS Multi-AZ db.t3.medium ~$120, ElastiCache Redis cache.t3.micro ~$25, ALB ~$30, S3 and data transfer ~$40, Cognito 12K MAU ~$9, CloudWatch Logs ~$80, Datadog APM ~$400. Stays within $2000/month constraint. Peak scaling during registration periods handled by ECS auto-scaling to 6 tasks.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (10 weeks): Cognito MFA auth, appointment booking with Redis-cached FHIR availability and circuit breaker, pre-visit intake form engine, push notification reminders, audit_log with device_id, Expo SecureStore for all credential storage, ECS Fargate deployment with WAF. Phase 2 (6 weeks): Secure provider-patient messaging via WebSocket, provider appointment queue with intake form review, visit summary from Epic FHIR Encounter resources, prescription refill request flow. Phase 3 (4 weeks): Telehealth video visit integration, immunization record viewing from FHIR Immunization resources, lab result push notifications, analytics dashboard for health services utilization metrics.
target_output.development_guidelines: Custom ESLint rule forbidding imports of @react-native-async-storage/async-storage in the project — all sensitive data goes through Expo SecureStore. PHI-touching endpoints must have integration tests with mock Epic FHIR responses using msw (Mock Service Worker). Every FHIR adapter method must have a unit test for circuit breaker open state behavior. HIPAA-mandated security review checklist required as a PR gate for any changes touching audit_log schema, PHI encryption, or Cognito configuration. TypeScript strict mode; no any types in PHI-handling modules. Biannual penetration testing with HIPAA-specific test cases.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Epic FHIR API changes breaking the adapter. Mitigation: opossum circuit breaker isolates failures; FHIR adapter versioned as a separate module with integration test suite against Epic sandbox. Risk: Push notification delivery failure causing patients to miss appointments. Mitigation: dead letter queue for failed notifications with SMS fallback via SNS for critical appointment reminders. Risk: PHI in Datadog APM traces despite masking rules. Mitigation: PHI field masking configured at Datadog agent level and validated in CI with a log sampling test that asserts no patient name or DOB patterns appear. Tradeoff: Redis availability caching introduces up to 15-minute stale data risk for booking. Mitigation: cache TTL set to 15 minutes matching Epic's typical slot update frequency; cache miss triggers synchronous FHIR fetch.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Confirmed Expo SecureStore over device-level encryption for per-field sensitive data control. Selected opossum circuit breaker over manual retry for Epic FHIR resilience due to configurable threshold and state machine clarity. Decided to encrypt PHI at application layer with AWS Encryption SDK in addition to KMS disk encryption for defense-in-depth. Epic FHIR sandbox credentials to be requested through university's existing Epic partnership agreement.

project_goal.value: Landing page hub for a multi-department university extension school where each academic department independently deploys its own microsite for professional development courses, with a shared header/footer component library and centralized analytics aggregation across all department subsites
project_goal.rationale: Departments need autonomy over their course listings while the extension school director needs unified enrollment interest analytics

target_users.value: Prospective professional development students browsing department course offerings; department marketing coordinators managing microsite content; extension school director reviewing aggregated enrollment analytics
target_users.rationale: Three distinct roles with different interaction patterns with the system

project_class.rationale: Static site architecture; no authenticated features or server-side runtime in microsites

capabilities.rationale: Static rendering with centralized analytics event aggregation across independent department deployments

complexity_level.rationale: Multi-repo microsite federation with shared npm component library adds coordination complexity above a simple landing page

risk_level.rationale: Public informational content only; no user accounts, payments, or sensitive data

data_sensitivity.rationale: Only aggregated analytics events with no PII; no form submissions stored on infrastructure

external_exposure.rationale: All department microsites are publicly accessible marketing content

access_model.value: No authentication for public visitors; department coordinators use Vercel dashboard directly for deployment management
access_model.rationale: No authenticated user features on public microsites

feature_scope.value: Hub index page listing all department microsites with course count and featured programs; per-department microsite with course listings, instructor profiles, and enrollment interest links; shared header/footer npm component library with university branding; centralized Vercel Analytics aggregation with department_id tagging
feature_scope.rationale: Covers all three user segment needs identified in discovery

mvp_scope.value: Three department microsites (Business, Technology, Healthcare) using shared component library; hub index page; analytics events tagged with department_id
mvp_scope.rationale: Three departments are ready to launch; others onboard in Phase 2

security_baseline.value: HTTPS enforced via Vercel; security headers (CSP, X-Frame-Options) via vercel.json headers config; no user data collected
security_baseline.rationale: Standard static site security headers sufficient; no authentication or data storage

frontend_stack.value: Next.js 14 with TypeScript (App Router, static export mode)
frontend_stack.rationale: Team familiarity; App Router supports shared layouts and component federation patterns

backend_stack.value: None -- fully static site; no server-side runtime
backend_stack.rationale: Landing page scope requires no backend API; static export eliminates server costs

data_platform.value: None -- course content managed as JSON/Markdown files in each department's git repository
data_platform.rationale: Content volume is small enough to manage in git; no CMS required for MVP

hosting_target.value: Vercel Edge Network; each department microsite deployed as a separate Vercel project under the extension school Vercel team
hosting_target.rationale: Separate Vercel projects enable independent deploy pipelines per department while sharing the same team analytics workspace

privacy_retention_policy.value: No personal data collected or stored; Vercel Analytics uses cookieless measurement respecting GDPR by default
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Cookieless analytics eliminates consent management requirements

future_scope.value: CMS integration (Sanity or Contentful) for non-technical coordinator content updates; course enrollment funnel integration with university SIS; additional department microsites (Arts, Sciences, Education)
future_scope.rationale: Phase 2 features pending MVP validation

constraints.value: Each department must be able to deploy independently without affecting other departments; shared component library version pinning must be enforced in CI to prevent visual inconsistencies
constraints.rationale: Department autonomy is a hard organizational requirement; branding consistency is a university policy requirement

observability_baseline.value: Vercel Analytics per project with custom department_id event property; extension school director views aggregated enrollment interest metrics in Vercel Analytics dashboard
observability_baseline.rationale: Built-in Vercel Analytics sufficient for enrollment interest measurement

execution_preference.value: Hub team owns component library; department teams own their microsites; coordination via versioned npm releases
execution_preference.rationale: Reflects existing organizational team structure

project.goal: Federated static microsite architecture for multi-department university extension school with shared branding component library
project.target_users: Prospective students, department coordinators, extension school director
project.timeline: Three department microsites live within 6 weeks

frontend.framework: Next.js 14 with TypeScript, App Router, output: export mode
frontend.styling: Tailwind CSS consumed from shared component library
frontend.components: @extension-school/ui npm package for Header, Footer, CourseCard, InstructorCard, HeroBanner

data.content: JSON and Markdown course content files in each department repository
data.analytics: Vercel Analytics with department_id custom property

devops.hosting: Vercel projects under extension school Vercel team
devops.environments: Preview per PR, production on main branch

constraints.versioning: Component library version pinned in CI

open_questions.cms: When to introduce CMS for non-technical coordinator content editing

reasoner_reviews.product: The multi-department microsite federation model correctly reflects the university's organizational structure where departments have publishing autonomy. The hub index page is important for discoverability -- prospective students should be able to browse all departments from a single entry point. The round 2 plan's analytics aggregation approach was correct but the component library versioning enforcement was underspecified; CI must block deployments using outdated major versions of @extension-school/ui to prevent visual drift.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Separate Vercel projects per department is the right isolation model; it enables independent preview environments and deploy pipelines. The @extension-school/ui npm package federated via GitHub Packages is the correct distribution mechanism. Round 2 did not specify the version constraint enforcement mechanism in CI -- add a step that reads the installed @extension-school/ui version from package.json and blocks build if it falls behind the minimum required major version published in a shared config file.
reasoner_reviews.security: No significant security concerns for a public static site with no authentication or data storage. Verify CSP header in vercel.json allows Vercel Analytics script domain. Ensure no enrollment interest tracking includes PII in event properties -- analytics events must contain only course_id, department_id, and anonymized session identifiers.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: The organizational constraint that departments deploy independently is satisfied by the separate Vercel project per department model. The branding consistency constraint requires the component library version pinning to be enforced automatically -- manual review is not reliable at scale when more departments are added.

frontend.app_structure: Hub repository: Next.js app with src/app/page.tsx (department directory), src/app/[department]/page.tsx (per-department overview). Each department repository: separate Next.js app with app/page.tsx (course catalog), app/courses/[slug]/page.tsx (course detail), app/instructors/page.tsx. Shared: @extension-school/ui npm package published to GitHub Packages
frontend.pages_and_flows: Hub index (/): grid of department cards (department name, course count, featured program, direct microsite link); individual department microsite: HeroBanner with department tagline, CourseCard grid (course title, duration, format online/hybrid/in-person, enrollment link), InstructorCard section, enrollment interest form CTA linking to university SIS course registration
frontend.performance_notes: Next.js output: 'export' generates fully static HTML; all pages pre-rendered at build time with no runtime server; Vercel CDN serves pages from edge nodes globally; Next.js Image optimization for instructor headshots and program photos

backend.service_design: No backend service. All content is static JSON/Markdown files resolved at build time via Next.js generateStaticParams. Enrollment links are external URLs to the university SIS course registration system.
backend.failure_handling: No runtime failure modes; fully static HTML files served from CDN

security.auth_design: No authentication -- fully public static site
security.abuse_prevention: Vercel DDoS protection at edge; no forms or interactive endpoints on microsites
security.privacy_controls: Vercel Analytics cookieless mode; no PII in analytics event properties; enrollment interest events contain only course_id, department_id, and anonymized visitor_id

data.entities: department_microsites (Git repo per department: name, slug, primary_color, tagline, feature_image), course_listings (JSON per department: course_id, title, description, duration_weeks, format ENUM online/hybrid/in_person, price_usd, enrollment_url, instructor_id), instructor_profiles (JSON per department: instructor_id, name, bio, photo_filename, expertise_areas[]), shared_component_library (@extension-school/ui: Header, Footer, CourseCard, InstructorCard, HeroBanner with typed props), analytics_events (Vercel Analytics: event_name, course_id, department_id, visitor_id_anonymized)
data.schema_notes: No database; all content as typed JSON files with Zod validation schemas in each department repo to catch malformed course data at build time

devops.deployment_topology: Vercel team: extension-school-hub project (hub index), extension-school-business project, extension-school-technology project, extension-school-healthcare project. Each project connected to its department GitHub repo. Separate production domains: extension.university.edu (hub), business.extension.university.edu, technology.extension.university.edu, healthcare.extension.university.edu
devops.ci_cd_design: Each department repo GitHub Actions workflow: check @extension-school/ui installed version against MINIMUM_COMPONENT_VERSION in shared .component-version config file (fail build if outdated major version), run next build, Vercel preview deploy on PR, Vercel production deploy on main push. Hub repo deploys independently on its own schedule.
devops.environments: Preview deployments per PR branch in every department repo; production on main branch merge; no staging environment needed for static content

target_output.thinking_summary: Round 3 revision for the university extension school microsite federation. The persistent medium-severity issue is the absence of a CI mechanism to enforce the @extension-school/ui component library version constraint. Fix by adding an explicit GitHub Actions version check step that reads the installed package version and compares it against a minimum_version field published in a shared @extension-school/component-config package. The hub index staleness issue (ISS-002) is low severity and can be addressed with a simple Vercel deploy hook trigger. No compliance context needed -- public static content only.

fix_report.action_taken: Added explicit CI version enforcement step to each department repo's GitHub Actions workflow: reads @extension-school/ui version from installed package.json, fetches minimum_allowed_major_version from @extension-school/component-config package at build time, fails with actionable error message if installed major version is below minimum; updated deployment_and_operations and development_guidelines sections to document this enforcement pattern
fix_report.expected_outcome: Departments cannot deploy outdated component library versions; branding consistency enforced automatically without manual coordinator review; CI failure message includes upgrade instructions

target_output.title: University Extension School Microsite Federation
target_output.executive_summary: A federated static microsite architecture for a multi-department university extension school, where each academic department (Business, Technology, Healthcare) independently deploys a Next.js 14 static site via Vercel while consuming a shared @extension-school/ui npm component library for consistent header, footer, and course listing components. The extension school hub index aggregates all department microsites in a single directory page. Vercel Analytics tracks enrollment interest events tagged with department_id for unified reporting. CI enforces minimum component library version constraints to maintain visual branding consistency across all department deployments.
target_output.architecture_overview: Multi-repo federated architecture: one hub repository and one repository per department, all deploying as separate Vercel projects under the extension school Vercel team. Each department repository is a Next.js 14 app with output set to 'export' for fully static generation. Shared UI components are distributed via the @extension-school/ui npm package published to GitHub Packages. Each department CI pipeline enforces a minimum @extension-school/ui major version before allowing deployment. Vercel Analytics is enabled on all projects with a custom department_id event property, allowing the extension school director to view enrollment interest aggregated across all departments in a single Vercel Analytics view. No backend runtime exists; enrollment links point to the university SIS registration system.
target_output.technology_stack: Frontend: Next.js 14 with TypeScript, App Router, output: 'export' (static generation). Shared UI: @extension-school/ui npm package (Tailwind CSS components: Header, Footer, CourseCard, InstructorCard, HeroBanner). Version Governance: @extension-school/component-config package exposing minimum_allowed_major_version field. Analytics: Vercel Analytics with custom event properties (department_id, course_id). Hosting: Vercel Edge Network, separate project per department. CI: GitHub Actions per department repo. Content: typed JSON course listings and Markdown instructor bios in each repo.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Hub Index (/): auto-generated grid of all department microsite cards (department name, course count, featured program title, direct link to department microsite), updated on each department deploy via Vercel deploy hook. Department Microsite: HeroBanner with department-specific tagline and hero image, CourseCard grid (course title, format badge online/hybrid/in-person, duration, enrollment CTA link to SIS), InstructorCard section with faculty bios. Shared Component Library: Header (university logo, global nav links, department name slot), Footer (university address, social links, sitemap), CourseCard (typed props: title, description, format, duration_weeks, price_usd, enrollment_url), InstructorCard (name, bio, photo, expertise_areas). Centralized Analytics: enrollment_interest_clicked events with course_id and department_id properties visible in aggregated Vercel Analytics view.
target_output.system_components: Vercel Edge CDN (serving all department microsite static HTML/CSS/images), Vercel Analytics (cookieless, aggregated across all department projects by department_id), @extension-school/ui npm package on GitHub Packages (Header, Footer, CourseCard, InstructorCard, HeroBanner Tailwind components), @extension-school/component-config npm package (minimum_allowed_major_version field for CI enforcement), GitHub Actions workflows per department repo (with version check step), Vercel deploy hooks (each department project triggers hub index rebuild on production deploy).
target_output.workflows: Department Microsite Deploy Workflow: department coordinator edits course JSON or Markdown files in their repository and pushes to main branch; GitHub Actions CI reads installed @extension-school/ui version from package.json, fetches minimum_allowed_major_version from @extension-school/component-config at build time, fails with 'Upgrade @extension-school/ui to major version X before deploying' if outdated, otherwise runs next build with output: export, deploys to Vercel production, and fires Vercel deploy hook to trigger hub index rebuild. Hub Index Rebuild: triggered by Vercel deploy hook from any department production deploy; hub Next.js build fetches each department's published course count from a static courses-manifest.json in each department's Vercel deployment, regenerates department grid page, deploys to hub Vercel project. New Department Onboarding: hub team scaffolds new department repo from template, publishes to GitHub, adds Vercel project to extension school team, adds deploy hook URL to department CI workflow, hub coordinator adds department entry to hub index source.
target_output.data_model: department_microsites (per-department repo config: department_id, name, slug, primary_color_hex, tagline, hero_image_filename, enrollment_base_url). course_listings (JSON array per department: course_id, title, short_description, long_description_md, format ENUM online/hybrid/in_person, duration_weeks INT, price_usd FLOAT, enrollment_url, instructor_id, published BOOLEAN). instructor_profiles (JSON array per department: instructor_id, name, title, bio_md, photo_filename, expertise_areas[]). shared_components (TypeScript interfaces in @extension-school/ui: CourseCardProps {course_id, title, format, duration_weeks, price_usd, enrollment_url}, InstructorCardProps {name, title, bio, photo_src, expertise_areas}). analytics_events (Vercel Analytics events: enrollment_interest_clicked {course_id: string, department_id: string}, department_page_viewed {department_id: string}). courses_manifest (static JSON in each Vercel deployment: {department_id, course_count, last_updated}).
target_output.api_design: No backend API. Content resolved at build time via Next.js generateStaticParams reading local JSON files. Analytics events sent client-side via Vercel Analytics track() function: track('enrollment_interest_clicked', {course_id: courseId, department_id: departmentId}). Hub index fetches courses-manifest.json from each department's Vercel deployment URL at build time (static data fetch in Next.js generateStaticProps). Deploy hooks: POST requests from Vercel department projects to hub Vercel deploy hook URL (configured as secret in each department repo). Enrollment links are external URLs to university SIS formatted as enrollment_base_url + '?course=' + course_id.
target_output.security_and_compliance: HTTPS enforced by Vercel for all projects. Security headers via vercel.json headers config on each project: Content-Security-Policy allowing Vercel Analytics script domain (va.vercel-scripts.com), X-Frame-Options DENY, X-Content-Type-Options nosniff, Referrer-Policy strict-origin-when-cross-origin. Vercel Analytics cookieless mode: no cookies set, no PII collected, GDPR-compliant by default. Analytics event properties contain only course_id and department_id -- no visitor name, email, or IP address. No forms or user inputs on any microsite; no attack surface beyond static file serving.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Vercel team: extension-school-hub project (hub.extension.university.edu), extension-school-business (business.extension.university.edu), extension-school-technology (technology.extension.university.edu), extension-school-healthcare (healthcare.extension.university.edu). Each department repo GitHub Actions workflow: step 1 -- read @extension-school/ui version from package.json; step 2 -- fetch minimum_allowed_major_version from @extension-school/component-config@latest; step 3 -- compare major versions, exit 1 with upgrade message if department version is lower; step 4 -- bun run build (next build); step 5 -- Vercel CLI deploy (preview on PR, production on main); step 6 on production -- curl Vercel hub deploy hook URL. Hub project: deploys on Vercel deploy hook trigger, fetching courses-manifest.json from each department's production deployment URL at build time.
target_output.observability: Vercel Analytics per project: page views, unique visitors, geography, device type. Custom events visible in Vercel Analytics under extension school Vercel team workspace: enrollment_interest_clicked (filterable by department_id and course_id), department_page_viewed (filterable by department_id). Extension school director views aggregated view showing which department courses attract the most enrollment interest. Vercel build logs accessible per project for deployment debugging. No server-side monitoring required for static sites.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Vercel Pro plan shared across all department projects under one team: approximately USD 20/month. Vercel Analytics included in Pro plan. GitHub Packages for @extension-school/ui: free tier (500MB storage, 1GB bandwidth/month). Total infrastructure cost: USD 20/month for all department microsites combined. CDN auto-scales globally to any traffic volume with no additional cost. Adding new departments requires only creating a new Vercel project -- no capacity planning.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (Weeks 1-4): Hub index page and three department microsites (Business, Technology, Healthcare) using shared @extension-school/ui v1.0 components; GitHub Actions CI with component version enforcement step; Vercel Analytics with department_id events; deploy hooks for hub index rebuild. Phase 2 (Weeks 5-8): Add four additional department microsites (Arts, Sciences, Education, Law) using same onboarding template; refine @extension-school/ui component library based on Phase 1 feedback; document department onboarding runbook. Phase 3 (Future): Introduce Sanity or Contentful headless CMS for non-technical coordinator content editing; course enrollment funnel integration with university SIS for real-time enrollment count display.
target_output.development_guidelines: All department repositories scaffold from a standard template maintained by the hub team. @extension-school/ui components must be consumed without modification -- no local overrides or wrapping components that duplicate shared styles. Tailwind class usage restricted to values defined in the shared design token file from @extension-school/ui. CI version check step is mandatory and cannot be bypassed. Course JSON files must pass Zod validation schema defined in @extension-school/ui at build time -- invalid course data fails the build before deployment. Analytics track() calls must only pass course_id and department_id as event properties -- no user-identifiable data.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: @extension-school/ui major version upgrade requires coordinated update across all department repos -- departments slow to upgrade will be blocked from deploying until they update. Mitigation: hub team provides upgrade migration guide and sets a 30-day grace period after publishing a new major version before enforcement is activated. Risk: hub index build fails if any department's courses-manifest.json URL is unreachable at build time. Mitigation: hub build uses try/catch on each department manifest fetch and falls back to last known course count from a cached manifest file. Tradeoff: static JSON course listings over a headless CMS sacrifices non-technical editing capability but keeps infrastructure cost minimal and eliminates CMS vendor dependency for the MVP launch.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Confirmed Vercel Analytics over self-hosted analytics (Plausible) to avoid separate infrastructure cost; Vercel Analytics cookieless mode satisfies GDPR without a consent banner. Confirmed separate Vercel projects over a monorepo with path-based routing to maintain department deployment independence -- a single Vercel project would make all departments share a deploy pipeline. Minimum component library major version enforcement window confirmed as 30 days after new major release.

metadata.notes: Multi-department university extension school microsite federation; round 3 fixes CI component library version enforcement mechanism that was missing from rounds 1-2.

profile.domain: University LMS CLI Tool — Bulk Course Management and Enrollment Automation for Instructional Designers

project_goal.value: A CLI tool enabling university instructional designers to interact programmatically with the campus LMS REST API for bulk course creation, module templating, content import from shared repositories, and enrollment management — replacing a tedious manual web UI workflow.
project_goal.rationale: Instructional designers managing hundreds of course shells per semester spend significant manual effort in the LMS UI; a CLI reduces this to scripted operations.

target_users.value: University instructional designers and LMS administrators who run scripts locally or on department servers.
target_users.rationale: Power users comfortable with command-line tools; no student-facing interaction.

project_class.rationale: Local command-line executable consuming LMS REST API — no server runtime, no browser UI.

capabilities.rationale: CLI command parsing, LMS API client, CSV/YAML input parsing — no frontend or database.

complexity_level.rationale: Single-process CLI with LMS API calls; no concurrency, no persistence beyond local log files.

risk_level.rationale: Handles internal course catalog data; no student PII beyond enrollment list lookups; LMS API access controlled by existing LMS permissions.

data_sensitivity.rationale: Course metadata and enrollment lists are internal university data, not student educational records requiring FERPA treatment in this context.

external_exposure.rationale: Tool runs on local workstations or department servers within university network; no external-facing endpoints.

access_model.value: LMS OAuth2 token stored in local ~/.lmscli/config.json or environment variable LMS_TOKEN; no multi-user authentication — each operator uses their own LMS OAuth2 credentials.
access_model.rationale: CLI tools use per-operator credentials consistent with LMS access control model.

feature_scope.value: Subcommands: course create (from YAML template), course list (with filters), module scaffold (applies template to existing course), content import (from shared repository by content_id), enrollment add/remove (from CSV), enrollment list. Dry-run flag for all mutating operations. JSON and table output modes. Rate limiting to avoid LMS API throttle.
feature_scope.rationale: Core operations that instructional designers perform repeatedly; dry-run prevents accidental bulk changes.

mvp_scope.value: course create, module scaffold, content import, enrollment add from CSV. Dry-run flag and JSON output mode in MVP.
mvp_scope.rationale: Highest-frequency operations that immediately reduce manual work for instructional designers.

security_baseline.value: OAuth2 token stored with file permissions 600; TLS 1.3 for LMS API calls; token never logged or printed to stdout; .lmscli/config.json excluded from shell history expansion.
security_baseline.rationale: Token compromise would grant LMS API access at the operator's permission level.

frontend_stack.value: none — CLI tool with no web UI; output rendered to stdout as formatted tables or JSON
frontend_stack.rationale: CLI tools output to terminal; no browser frontend.

backend_stack.value: Node.js 20 with Bun runtime; commander.js for CLI argument parsing; node-fetch for LMS API HTTP calls
backend_stack.rationale: Instructional design team is familiar with JavaScript/TypeScript; Bun provides fast startup for CLI use.

data_platform.value: No database — stateless CLI. Local YAML/JSON config files for templates and settings. Bulk operation logs written to local ./lmscli-audit.log.
data_platform.rationale: CLI tools should be stateless; template files live in version control alongside scripts.

hosting_target.value: Distributed as an npm package (private university registry); runs on local macOS/Linux workstations and department Linux servers.
hosting_target.rationale: npm package distribution is the standard for Node.js CLI tools; no server hosting required.

privacy_retention_policy.value: Audit logs stored locally for 30 days; no centralized log collection; enrollment CSVs are operator-managed inputs and not retained by the tool.
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Internal tool with local logging; no centralized data retention obligations.

future_scope.value: Plugin architecture for additional LMS integrations; configuration profiles for multiple LMS instances; batch job mode for scheduled overnight course provisioning.
future_scope.rationale: University has multiple LMS instances; plugin system enables reuse across departments.

constraints.value: LMS API rate limit: 100 requests/min per OAuth2 token. Must support dry-run mode for all mutating commands. YAML template files must be human-editable without programming knowledge.
constraints.rationale: Instructional designers are not developers; YAML templates must be approachable.

observability_baseline.value: Structured JSON output to ./lmscli-audit.log for all API calls (command, endpoint, status_code, duration_ms, dry_run flag). --verbose flag enables debug output to stderr.
observability_baseline.rationale: Local audit log enables post-hoc review of bulk operations.

execution_preference.value: Single-binary distribution via npm; TypeScript compiled to CJS with tsc; ESM imports where possible; no build step required for development (Bun runs TS directly).
execution_preference.rationale: Fast development iteration with Bun; production binary compiled for npm distribution.

project.goal: CLI for bulk LMS course management and enrollment automation
project.target_users: Instructional designers and LMS administrators
project.timeline: Ship before fall semester course setup period

backend.framework: commander.js for CLI parsing; node-fetch for LMS API

security.auth: LMS OAuth2 token per operator
security.encryption: TLS 1.3 for LMS API calls; config file permissions 600

reasoner_reviews.product: Scope is appropriate for the use case. The dry-run flag is a safety-critical feature for instructional designers who may accidentally apply templates to the wrong course shells — it must be implemented as a pre-execution check that logs the planned API calls without executing them. JSON output mode enables composability with shell scripts and downstream automation.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: commander.js is the right CLI framework for this complexity level; yargs would be overkill. Rate limiting with a token bucket (100 req/min) should be implemented as a thin wrapper around node-fetch, not a third-party library. YAML template schema validation at parse time (before any API calls) prevents partial execution failures mid-batch.
reasoner_reviews.security: OAuth2 token must never appear in process arguments (visible via ps aux) — must be read from config file or environment variable only. The config file at ~/.lmscli/config.json should be created with 600 permissions by the init command and rejected if world-readable on startup.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Rate limiting at exactly 100 req/min needs a sliding window or token bucket approach — simple sleep-based throttling will underperform. Recommend the p-throttle library or a hand-rolled token bucket using setInterval. YAML template validation against a JSON Schema before any API calls prevents partial operations.

backend.service_design: CLI entry point at src/cli.ts, registered as 'lmscli' binary in package.json bin field. Command modules: CourseCommand (course create/list/clone), ModuleCommand (module scaffold/list), ContentCommand (content import/list), EnrollmentCommand (enrollment add/remove/list). Each command module exports a commander.Command instance. Top-level program.js composes all command modules and adds global flags (--dry-run, --output json|table, --profile). LmsApiClient class wraps node-fetch with token injection, TLS enforcement, retry (3x exponential backoff), and rate limiting (token bucket, 100 req/min).
backend.api_patterns: Internal LmsApiClient methods: createCourse(templatePayload), scaffoldModules(courseId, moduleTemplate[]), importContent(courseId, contentId), addEnrollment(courseId, userId, role), removeEnrollment(courseId, userId). All methods return typed response objects. Dry-run mode returns a DryRunResult with planned_api_calls[] instead of executing.
backend.failure_handling: LMS API 429 responses trigger automatic wait-and-retry using Retry-After header value. LMS API 4xx errors (except 429) are non-retryable — logged to audit log and reported to stderr with the failed item identifier. Batch operations continue on per-item failure and report a summary of successes and failures at completion. Schema validation errors in YAML templates abort before any API calls.

security.auth_design: OAuth2 access token loaded from LMS_TOKEN environment variable or ~/.lmscli/config.json token field. Config file checked for world-readable permissions on startup — process exits with error if permissions are not 600 or 400. Token injected as Authorization: Bearer header by LmsApiClient — never passed as URL parameter or written to audit log.
security.secrets_management: lmscli init command creates ~/.lmscli/config.json with 600 permissions and prompts for LMS instance URL and initial OAuth2 token. No token refresh flow — operators renew tokens manually via LMS UI. Token expiry handled by LMS API 401 response, which surfaces a clear re-authentication error message.
security.audit_and_logging_controls: Structured JSON lines written to ./lmscli-audit.log: {timestamp, command, subcommand, lms_endpoint, http_method, status_code, duration_ms, dry_run, item_count, success_count, error_count}. Token value excluded from all log entries. --verbose flag writes debug lines to stderr only.

data.entities: lms_courses (in-memory during command execution: course_id, course_name, lms_course_id, created_at), course_templates (YAML files: name, course_code_prefix, default_modules[], default_settings), module_templates (YAML: module_name, items[], publish_status), enrollment_records (CSV input: student_id, section_code, role), bulk_operation_logs (local JSONL: command, timestamp, status, item_count).
data.schema_notes: Course template YAML validated against JSON Schema on load. Enrollment CSV validated for required columns (student_id, section_code) before batch processing. No persistent database — all state is transient within a single CLI invocation.

devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: ESLint + tsc --noEmit type check, bun test for unit tests (mocked LmsApiClient), bun build for npm package compilation. On tag push: npm publish to university private registry. Integration tests use a local LMS sandbox API mock server.
devops.observability_stack: No server-side observability. Local audit log at ./lmscli-audit.log. --verbose flag for debug output. npm pack dry-run in CI to verify package contents.
devops.rollback_strategy: npm dist-tags allow rollback to previous version via npm dist-tag add lmscli@{version} latest. Operators pin versions in department run-scripts.

ISS-001.title: Rate limiting implementation not specified — risk of LMS API 429 storms during bulk enrollment
ISS-001.detail: Round 1 plan described rate limiting as needed but did not specify the algorithm (token bucket vs leaky bucket vs sleep-based throttle). LMS API throttle at 100 req/min with enrollment batches of 500+ students requires a proper token bucket, not naive sleep.

history.note: Missing from initial plan's LmsApiClient design.

focus_issues.title: Rate limiting implementation not specified — risk of LMS API 429 storms during bulk enrollment
focus_issues.detail: Round 1 plan described rate limiting as needed but did not specify the algorithm. LMS API at 100 req/min with 500+ student enrollment batches requires a token bucket, not naive sleep.

latest_recommendations: Specify token bucket algorithm for LMS API rate limiting in LmsApiClient with configurable bucket size and refill rate matching LMS vendor spec of 100 req/min.
revision_memory.latest_plan_title: LMS CLI Tool — Round 1

convergence_state.reason: ISS-001 unresolved from Round 1.

previous_audits.summary: Plan is structurally appropriate for a CLI tool but the LmsApiClient rate limiting design is underspecified — the algorithm is mentioned but not defined, which is a medium risk for bulk operations against a throttled LMS API.
strengths: CLI command structure with commander.js is well-organized
Dry-run mode correctly described as pre-execution API call plan
YAML template schema validation before API calls prevents partial failures
concerns: Rate limiting algorithm unspecified — token bucket vs sleep-based not decided
Token bucket implementation detail missing from LmsApiClient spec
recommendations: Add token bucket implementation detail to LmsApiClient: bucket capacity=100, refill rate=100/60s, implemented with setInterval and a counter; or use p-throttle(100, 60000) as a wrapper

issue_updates.title: Rate limiting implementation not specified
issue_updates.detail: LmsApiClient design section says rate limiting is needed but does not specify algorithm.

previous_plan.title: LMS CLI Tool — Round 1
previous_plan.executive_summary: A Node.js/Bun CLI tool with commander.js for bulk LMS course management, module templating, and enrollment automation. LmsApiClient wraps LMS API with retry and rate limiting. Dry-run mode for all mutating commands.
previous_plan.architecture_overview: Single-process CLI binary. commander.js command tree. LmsApiClient with TLS, OAuth2 token injection, retry. YAML template validation. Local audit log.
previous_plan.security_and_compliance: Token from env var or config file with 600 permissions. Token excluded from logs. Rate limiting to avoid 429s.

target_output.thinking_summary: Round 2 must address ISS-001 by specifying the token bucket algorithm for LMS API rate limiting. However, this plan has intentional flaws: a frontend specialist subplan is included despite cli_tool project class (FrontendAgent would not run for cli_tool), and the system_components section incorrectly references a web dashboard that conflicts with the CLI-only contract. The fix_report will address ISS-001 with a token bucket specification, but the structural inconsistencies remain as the intentional quality issues.

fix_report.action_taken: Specified token bucket rate limiting in LmsApiClient: bucket capacity of 100 tokens, refill rate of 100 tokens per 60 seconds using a setInterval-based counter. Requests consume 1 token; if bucket is empty, request is queued until next refill tick. Implementation uses p-throttle(100, 60000) as the wrapper function around fetch calls.
fix_report.expected_outcome: ISS-001 resolved — LmsApiClient will not exceed 100 req/min even during 500+ student enrollment batches.

target_output.title: LMS CLI Tool — Bulk Course Provisioning and Enrollment Automation for Instructional Designers
target_output.executive_summary: A TypeScript CLI tool built on Node.js 20 with Bun runtime, distributed via the university private npm registry, enabling instructional designers to bulk-create course shells from YAML templates, scaffold module structures, import shared content by content_id, and manage enrollments from CSV files — replacing per-course web UI workflows. commander.js command tree exposes course, module, content, and enrollment subcommands with a global --dry-run flag. LmsApiClient wraps the LMS REST API with OAuth2 token injection, TLS enforcement, token bucket rate limiting (100 req/min), and exponential retry.
target_output.architecture_overview: Single-process CLI binary registered as 'lmscli' in package.json bin field. Entry point at src/cli.ts composing four command modules (CourseCommand, ModuleCommand, ContentCommand, EnrollmentCommand) under a root commander program. LmsApiClient provides all LMS API interactions with a p-throttle(100, 60000) rate limiter wrapping each fetch call. YAML template files validated against JSON Schema at parse time before any API calls execute. Dry-run mode constructs a DryRunPlan[] of planned API calls and writes them to stdout without executing. Audit log written to ./lmscli-audit.log in JSONL format. OAuth2 token loaded from LMS_TOKEN env var or ~/.lmscli/config.json (permissions checked for 600 on startup). Distributed as a compiled CJS package via npm publish to the university private registry.
target_output.technology_stack: Runtime: Node.js 20 with Bun 1.1 for development and type-checking. Language: TypeScript 5.4 (compiled to CJS for npm distribution). CLI framework: commander.js 12. HTTP client: node-fetch 3 wrapped with p-throttle 5 (token bucket, 100 req/min). YAML parsing: js-yaml 4 with JSON Schema validation via ajv 8. Output formatting: cli-table3 for table mode; JSON.stringify for JSON mode. Distribution: npm package published to university Verdaccio registry. CI: GitHub Actions with bun test and tsc --noEmit.
target_output.functional_feature_map: course create --template <yaml_file> [--dry-run]: reads course template YAML, validates schema, calls POST /api/v1/courses for each course definition. course list [--term <id>] [--output json|table]: calls GET /api/v1/courses with query filters, renders results. module scaffold --course <id> --template <yaml_file> [--dry-run]: applies module template to existing course via POST /api/v1/courses/{id}/modules for each module entry. content import --course <id> --content-id <id> [--dry-run]: calls POST /api/v1/courses/{id}/content_migrations with source_id. enrollment add --course <id> --csv <file> [--dry-run]: reads CSV, validates columns, POSTs enrollment records in batches of 50 with rate limiting. enrollment remove --course <id> --csv <file> [--dry-run]: DELETEs enrollments matching CSV user IDs.
target_output.system_components: lmscli binary (commander.js CLI, Node.js 20/Bun runtime), LmsApiClient module (p-throttle rate limiter, node-fetch, retry wrapper, TLS enforcement), CourseCommand/ModuleCommand/ContentCommand/EnrollmentCommand modules, YamlTemplateLoader (js-yaml + ajv validation), DryRunPlanner (generates planned_api_calls[] without executing), AuditLogger (JSONL writer to ./lmscli-audit.log), ConfigLoader (reads ~/.lmscli/config.json, enforces 600 permissions). Web dashboard (React 18 SPA served from S3/CloudFront for course management overview — planned for Phase 2).
target_output.workflows: Bulk Course Creation: instructional designer runs 'lmscli course create --template fall2026-courses.yaml --dry-run'; YamlTemplateLoader validates template schema, DryRunPlanner outputs JSON list of planned POST /api/v1/courses calls; designer reviews and re-runs without --dry-run; LmsApiClient executes each course creation call through p-throttle limiter; audit log records each call outcome. Enrollment Batch Upload: designer runs 'lmscli enrollment add --course CS101-F26 --csv enrollments.csv'; EnrollmentCommand reads CSV, validates student_id and section_code columns, batches 50 records at a time, LmsApiClient POSTs each batch through rate limiter; on LMS 429 response Retry-After header triggers automatic wait; completion summary printed to stdout with success_count and error_count.
target_output.data_model: In-memory during execution: lms_courses (course_id, course_name, lms_course_id, created_at), lms_modules (module_id, course_id, module_name, position, publish_status), enrollment_records (student_id, section_code, role, processed BOOL). Local files: course_templates (YAML: name, course_code_prefix, default_modules[], default_settings{}), module_templates (YAML: module_name, items[], publish_status), template_definitions (YAML schema in src/schemas/course-template.schema.json, module-template.schema.json). Audit log: bulk_operation_logs (JSONL: {timestamp, command, subcommand, lms_endpoint, http_method, status_code, duration_ms, dry_run, item_count, success_count, error_count}).
target_output.api_design: Internal LmsApiClient interface: createCourse(payload: CoursePayload): Promise<LmsCourse>, scaffoldModules(courseId: string, modules: ModuleTemplate[]): Promise<LmsModule[]>, importContent(courseId: string, contentId: string): Promise<ContentMigration>, addEnrollment(courseId: string, userId: string, role: string): Promise<Enrollment>, removeEnrollment(courseId: string, userId: string): Promise<void>, listCourses(filters: CourseFilter): Promise<LmsCourse[]>. All methods wrapped by p-throttle(100, 60000) rate limiter. CLI output: --output json returns raw API response array; --output table (default) renders cli-table3 formatted output to stdout.
target_output.security_and_compliance: OAuth2 token loaded exclusively from LMS_TOKEN env var or ~/.lmscli/config.json; config file checked for fs.statSync permissions on startup — process.exit(1) with clear message if permissions are not 600 or 400. Token injected as 'Authorization: Bearer {token}' header by LmsApiClient; token value excluded from all audit log entries and never printed to stdout or stderr. TLS 1.3 enforced by node-fetch (rejectUnauthorized: true, minVersion: TLSv1.2). No student PII stored persistently — enrollment CSVs are operator inputs and not retained after command execution.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: GitHub Actions CI: ESLint + tsc --noEmit type check, bun test for unit tests with mocked LmsApiClient responses, integration tests against a local LMS sandbox API mock (nock or msw), npm pack dry-run to verify package contents. Release pipeline: on git tag push, bun build compiles TypeScript to CJS dist/, npm publish to university Verdaccio private registry. Operators install via 'npm install -g @university/lmscli'. Version pinning: department run-scripts specify exact version. Rollback: npm dist-tag add @university/lmscli@{prev_version} latest. No server infrastructure to deploy or monitor — fully local execution.
target_output.observability: Local audit log ./lmscli-audit.log in JSONL format: {timestamp, command, subcommand, lms_endpoint, http_method, status_code, duration_ms, dry_run, item_count, success_count, error_count}. Log rotated by operator (no automatic rotation — simple append-only file). --verbose flag writes debug lines including request headers (token redacted) to stderr. Exit code 0 on full success, 1 on partial failure, 2 on configuration error — enables shell script error handling. No server-side metrics collection.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Zero hosting cost — CLI runs locally. npm private registry (Verdaccio) runs on existing university infrastructure at negligible cost. Development tooling (GitHub Actions CI) uses free tier. Scaling is per-operator: each instructional designer runs their own CLI instance with their own OAuth2 token, each subject to the 100 req/min LMS rate limit independently.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (3 weeks): LmsApiClient with p-throttle rate limiting and retry, CourseCommand (create, list), EnrollmentCommand (add from CSV), YAML template loader with schema validation, dry-run mode, audit log, lmscli init command for config setup, GitHub Actions CI with bun test. Phase 2 (2 weeks): ModuleCommand (scaffold), ContentCommand (import from shared repository), enrollment remove, --output table mode with cli-table3, integration tests against LMS sandbox mock. Phase 3 (2 weeks): config profile support for multiple LMS instances, plugin interface for additional LMS vendor adapters, npm package publishing pipeline to university Verdaccio registry.
target_output.development_guidelines: TypeScript strict mode with no implicit any. All LmsApiClient methods return typed result objects — never raw fetch Response. YAML template files must validate against JSON Schema before any API calls; validation errors abort with a human-readable error message listing each schema violation. Dry-run mode must produce identical output format to live mode but with dry_run: true in audit log and a DRY RUN prefix in stdout. Unit tests mock LmsApiClient — no real LMS API calls in CI. Token value must not appear in any string that is logged, printed, or included in error messages.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: LMS API schema changes break YAML template validation. Mitigation: JSON Schema files versioned alongside CLI; lmscli upgrade command can pull updated schemas from university schema registry. Tradeoff: p-throttle token bucket operates per CLI process — if two instructional designers run simultaneous bulk operations under the same OAuth2 token, combined throughput exceeds 100 req/min and triggers LMS throttling. Mitigation: recommend per-operator tokens. Risk: Large enrollment CSV (5,000 students) takes 50 minutes at 100 req/min. Mitigation: batch endpoint support in Phase 2 if LMS vendor exposes bulk enrollment API.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Rate limiting: p-throttle(100, 60000) token bucket confirmed — wraps each LmsApiClient fetch call, bucket capacity 100 tokens, refill 100 tokens per 60 seconds, queues excess requests without dropping them. Distribution: university Verdaccio private npm registry confirmed over GitHub Packages for institutional control. YAML over JSON for templates confirmed — more human-editable for non-developer instructional designers.

profile.domain: CS Department Serverless Code Grader Research Prototype

project_goal.value: Research prototype for a CS department exploring whether serverless infrastructure can reduce per-student compute cost of automated code graders for introductory programming courses compared to always-on VMs, by executing student code submissions in isolated ephemeral Lambda with Firecracker microVMs
project_goal.rationale: Department pays $3200/month for always-on EC2 grader VMs; hypothesis is serverless reduces this to under $800/month at same throughput

target_users.value: CS department faculty and the research team running the prototype; not a production student-facing system
target_users.rationale: Internal research tool operated by department IT and faculty, not directly accessed by students in the prototype phase

project_class.rationale: Exploratory research system; not production-grade; results feed academic paper, not operational deployment

capabilities.rationale: Serverless batch execution, DynamoDB for grading results, cost analytics, no frontend needed for research phase

complexity_level.rationale: Lambda orchestration with Firecracker isolation and cost tracking adds meaningful complexity over a simple grader

risk_level.rationale: Student code execution requires strong sandbox isolation to prevent cross-submission data leakage; prototype handles internal data only

data_sensitivity.rationale: Prototype uses anonymized student submission samples; not linked to individual student records for research phase

external_exposure.rationale: Research prototype accessible only within university AWS account; no public endpoints

access_model.value: AWS IAM roles for research team members; Lambda invoked via AWS CLI or research scripts; no web-based auth layer
access_model.rationale: Research tool invoked programmatically by research team; IAM is sufficient for internal-only access

feature_scope.value: Submit code for grading via Lambda invocation, execute against test suite in Firecracker microVM sandbox, return pass/fail with execution time and memory metrics, cost tracking per submission vs EC2 baseline, weekly cost comparison reports
feature_scope.rationale: Core research question requires both grading correctness validation and cost measurement

mvp_scope.value: Lambda grading function executing Python and Java submissions against predefined test suites in Firecracker isolation, DynamoDB result storage, cost metrics from CloudWatch Lambda billing data
mvp_scope.rationale: Python and Java cover 90% of CS1/CS2 submission volume; cost comparison is the primary research output

security_baseline.value: Firecracker microVM isolation for code execution; no network access from sandboxes; Lambda execution role with minimal IAM permissions; DynamoDB at-rest encryption
security_baseline.rationale: Code execution sandbox isolation is the primary security requirement to prevent student code from accessing other submissions

frontend_stack.value: none — research prototype invoked via AWS CLI and Python research scripts; no web UI
frontend_stack.rationale: Research prototype class; no browser UI required

backend_stack.rationale: Go compiles to small Lambda binaries with fast cold starts; suitable for latency-sensitive grading invocations

data_platform.value: DynamoDB for grading results and cost metrics; S3 for submission artifacts and test case bundles
data_platform.rationale: DynamoDB handles high write throughput during batch grading runs; S3 stores submission code and test bundles cheaply

hosting_target.value: AWS Lambda with Firecracker microVMs in university AWS account
hosting_target.rationale: Lambda is the serverless infrastructure being evaluated for cost reduction research

privacy_retention_policy.value: Anonymized submission samples retained for duration of research project (estimated 12 months); cost metrics retained permanently for research paper data; DynamoDB tables deleted at project conclusion
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Research data retention aligned with IRB approval scope and academic research data policies

future_scope.value: If cost hypothesis is validated, production deployment plan for all CS department courses; C++ and JavaScript language support; integration with Canvas LMS submission webhooks
future_scope.rationale: Research findings will determine whether to invest in production-grade deployment

constraints.value: Lambda execution timeout 15 minutes maximum; Firecracker available on Lambda via custom runtime; no per-submission cost to exceed $0.05; research budget $500/month total AWS spend
constraints.rationale: Lambda timeout bounds maximum grading job duration; cost constraint defines acceptable serverless economics

observability_baseline.value: CloudWatch Lambda metrics for duration, memory, and cold starts; DynamoDB cost tracking table updated after each grading job; weekly cost comparison report generated by analytics Lambda
observability_baseline.rationale: Cost measurement is the primary research output; CloudWatch provides Lambda billing granularity needed for per-submission cost tracking

execution_preference.value: Iterative research sprints; prototype results reviewed by faculty advisor biweekly
execution_preference.rationale: Research prototype development follows academic research cycle with biweekly advisor reviews

project.goal: Validate serverless cost hypothesis: Lambda+Firecracker grading at <$0.05/submission vs $0.12/submission on EC2
project.target_users: CS department research team and faculty advisor
project.timeline: 12-week research sprint for initial cost comparison results

backend.framework: AWS Lambda with custom Firecracker runtime layer
backend.grading: Test suite execution in Firecracker microVM with configurable memory and timeout limits

security.isolation: Firecracker microVM per submission, no network access, read-only filesystem except /tmp
security.iam: Lambda execution role with DynamoDB and S3 write only, no EC2 or broader AWS access

data.db: DynamoDB for grading_results and cost_metrics tables
data.storage: S3 for submission artifacts and test_case bundles

devops.ci: GitHub Actions for Lambda build and SAM deploy

reasoner_reviews.product: The cost comparison research question is well-defined. The control group (EC2 baseline) must use the same test suites and submission samples as the Lambda group for valid cost comparison. The weekly cost report Lambda should aggregate both Lambda invocation costs from CloudWatch and EC2 instance-hour costs from Cost Explorer for a fair comparison.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Go is the right Lambda runtime for cold start sensitivity in grading workloads. Firecracker custom runtime layer adds Lambda cold start latency of 200-400ms which should be measured and reported as a research finding. DynamoDB on-demand mode is cost-efficient for the bursty batch grading traffic pattern. S3 for submission bundles avoids Lambda payload size limits for large test suites.
reasoner_reviews.security: Firecracker microVM isolation is the correct sandbox model for code execution. The Lambda execution IAM role must be scoped to write-only on the specific DynamoDB table and S3 bucket — no read access to other submissions. Execution environment must deny network access from within the microVM to prevent exfiltration of other submissions via outbound HTTP.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: The $500/month budget requires careful Lambda memory configuration. At 512 MB Lambda memory with 10-second average execution: 1000 submissions/day costs approximately $0.021/submission in Lambda charges. At 5000 submissions/day during exam periods the cost approaches the $0.05 limit — this is a research finding to document.

backend.service_design: Two Lambda functions: grader Lambda (Go binary, custom Firecracker runtime layer, invoked per submission) and analytics Lambda (Go binary, triggered by CloudWatch Events weekly, reads DynamoDB to produce cost comparison report). SAM template defines both functions with appropriate memory and timeout configurations.
backend.api_patterns: Lambda invoked via AWS SDK InvokeFunction: input payload {submission_id, language ENUM python/java, code_s3_key, test_suite_s3_key, memory_mb, timeout_seconds}. Response: {submission_id, passed BOOLEAN, test_results [{test_name, passed, output}], duration_ms, memory_used_mb, lambda_billing_duration_ms}. Analytics Lambda produces JSON report written to S3.
backend.failure_handling: Lambda retry policy: 0 retries on grading Lambda to prevent duplicate grading charges. Dead letter queue (SQS) captures failed invocations for research team inspection. Grading timeout enforced at Firecracker level (SIGKILL at timeout_seconds) independent of Lambda timeout.

data.entities: grading_jobs (PK: submission_id, SK: 'JOB', language, code_s3_key, test_suite_id, status ENUM pending/running/completed/failed, submitted_at, completed_at, TTL). grading_results (PK: submission_id, SK: 'RESULT', passed BOOLEAN, test_results_json STRING, duration_ms NUMBER, memory_used_mb NUMBER, lambda_duration_billed_ms NUMBER, lambda_cost_usd NUMBER, created_at). cost_metrics (PK: date YYYY-MM-DD, SK: metric_name, total_submissions NUMBER, total_cost_usd NUMBER, avg_cost_per_submission NUMBER, p95_duration_ms NUMBER, cold_start_count NUMBER). ec2_baseline (PK: date YYYY-MM-DD, SK: 'EC2_COST', instance_type, hourly_cost, grading_jobs_run, cost_per_submission NUMBER).
data.storage_design: DynamoDB on-demand mode for bursty batch grading traffic. S3 bucket structure: s3://cs-grader-research/submissions/{submission_id}/code.zip, s3://cs-grader-research/test-suites/{suite_id}/tests.tar.gz, s3://cs-grader-research/reports/weekly_{date}.json. DynamoDB TTL on grading_jobs table set to 90 days for automatic cleanup.

devops.deployment_topology: AWS SAM template defining grader Lambda (Go, arm64, 512 MB, 60s timeout), analytics Lambda (Go, arm64, 256 MB, 5 min timeout), DynamoDB tables, S3 bucket, CloudWatch Events rule for weekly analytics. Deployed to single us-east-1 research environment.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: go test ./..., go build for Lambda binary, sam build, sam deploy --no-confirm-changeset. Manual SAM deploy for research prototype; no staging environment needed.

ISS-001.title: Grading Lambda IAM role has overly broad S3 permissions allowing cross-submission read access
ISS-001.detail: Round 1 Lambda execution role granted s3:GetObject on the entire research bucket. This allows a malicious student submission to exfiltrate other students code files if it escapes the Firecracker microVM. Fixed in round 2 by scoping S3 GetObject permission to only the specific submission prefix via IAM condition.

history.note: IAM policy updated with S3 prefix condition

ISS-002.title: cost_metrics DynamoDB table missing EC2 baseline data making cost comparison impossible
ISS-002.detail: Rounds 1 and 2 built Lambda cost tracking but never implemented the EC2 baseline cost capture. The research hypothesis requires a valid comparison group. Without EC2 baseline data in DynamoDB the weekly cost report has no denominator for the cost reduction claim.

history.note: EC2 baseline data collection still missing from analytics Lambda

ISS-003.detail: The weekly report is written to S3 as JSON but faculty advisor and research team have no easy way to view it without AWS console access. A simple email delivery of the report summary was requested but not implemented.

focus_issues.detail: Research hypothesis requires valid comparison group. Without EC2 baseline data the weekly cost report has no denominator.

focus_issues.title: Analytics Lambda produces cost report in S3 but no faculty-accessible delivery mechanism
focus_issues.detail: Weekly report written to S3 as JSON but research team has no easy viewing mechanism without AWS console.

Add SES email delivery of the weekly cost comparison report HTML summary to the faculty advisor and research team distribution list

target_output.thinking_summary: Round 3 revision of the serverless code grader research prototype. The two remaining issues block the core research question: ISS-002 (no EC2 baseline data) makes cost comparison impossible; ISS-003 (no report delivery) makes findings inaccessible to the faculty advisor. Fixing ISS-002 by adding AWS Cost Explorer API calls in the analytics Lambda to fetch daily EC2 grader instance costs. Fixing ISS-003 by adding SES email delivery of the weekly report HTML to a configured recipient list. These are self-contained additions to the analytics Lambda with no changes to the grading Lambda or data schema.
