previous_plan.title: Open-Source LLM Essay Scoring Prototype Round 1
previous_plan.executive_summary: A research prototype on Hetzner bare-metal GPU hardware running Llama 3 and Mistral via Ollama to score student essays against rubrics and benchmark cost against commercial scoring APIs.
previous_plan.data_model: scoring_runs, student_essays, evaluations (no model_id column -- all model results in one table), human_graded_samples, cost_benchmarks.
previous_plan.workflows: Essay batch upload and LLM scoring via Ollama with aggregate IRR calculation. No checkpoint recovery mechanism described.
previous_plan.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: LLM output format inconsistency requiring re-prompting. Risk: Ollama crash loses partial batch with no recovery. No mention of model_id isolation for cross-model comparison.

target_output.thinking_summary: Round 2 revision for the LLM essay scoring research prototype. The blocking issue is the data schema not isolating model_evaluation results by model_id -- without this, the core research output (per-model accuracy vs commercial APIs) cannot be computed from stored data. Fix by adding model_id as a NOT NULL FK on model_evaluations and creating a models reference table. Also add per-essay checkpoint writes to SQLite in the scoring orchestrator to handle Ollama crashes during long 70B batch runs. The local-only LLM constraint on Hetzner bare-metal and IRB/FERPA data requirements shape all other decisions.

fix_report.action_taken: Added models reference table (id, name, ollama_tag, parameter_count_b, quantization_level) and made model_evaluations.model_id a NOT NULL FK referencing models; updated data_model section with full partitioned schema; updated workflows to show per-model scoring run isolation and per-model IRR computation queries; updated system_components to include models table in SQLite schema
fix_report.expected_outcome: Each model's scoring results are independently queryable; statistical IRR and accuracy comparisons between Llama 3-8B, Llama 3-70B, and Mistral 7B are valid and non-ambiguous; paper results tables can be generated directly from SQL GROUP BY model_id queries

target_output.title: Open-Source LLM Essay Scoring Prototype Round 2
target_output.executive_summary: A FERPA-aware research prototype on a Hetzner bare-metal RTX 4090 server scoring anonymized student essays with locally-hosted open-source LLMs (Llama 3-8B, Llama 3-70B, Mistral 7B via Ollama) against configurable rubrics. Computes per-model inter-rater reliability against human gold-standard grades and produces cost-per-essay benchmarks comparing commodity GPU compute against commercial scoring API pricing. All essay data is pseudonymized under IRB protocol and accessed only through university VPN. Round 2 fixes model_id isolation in model_evaluations to enable valid cross-model statistical comparison.
target_output.architecture_overview: Single-server architecture on Hetzner AX102 bare-metal (RTX 4090, 64GB DDR5) with nginx handling TLS termination and routing to FastAPI scoring backend and Streamlit researcher dashboard. Ollama serves quantized open-source LLMs on localhost port 11434; FastAPI submits per-essay inference requests with rubric-aware chain-of-thought prompts and checkpoints each result to SQLite immediately after parsing, with model_id as a NOT NULL column on every evaluation record. Prometheus and NVIDIA DCGM Exporter capture GPU utilization and power draw for cost benchmarking. VPN-only access enforces the internal_only exposure requirement per IRB data governance.
target_output.technology_stack: Backend: Python 3.12, FastAPI 0.111, Ollama (Llama 3-8B Q4_K_M, Llama 3-70B Q4_K_M, Mistral 7B Q4_K_M), SQLAlchemy ORM, Alembic migrations. Frontend: Streamlit 1.35 multi-page researcher dashboard. Infrastructure: nginx 1.24 (TLS 1.3, JWT validation), systemd service management, Hetzner AX102. Observability: Prometheus 2.x, Grafana 10, NVIDIA DCGM Exporter, python-prometheus-client. CI: GitHub Actions with ruff and pytest.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Essay Batch Ingest: upload TXT/DOCX files, strip authorship metadata via PyMuPDF, assign pseudonymous_student_id from HMAC-SHA256 of IRB participant code, persist to student_essays. Rubric Configuration: JSON rubric editor with named dimensions (thesis_clarity, evidence_quality, argumentation_depth, mechanics), point scales per dimension, and dimension weights for composite scoring. Scoring Run Orchestration: select model and rubric, submit batch to FastAPI async queue, per-essay Ollama inference with checkpoint writes after each result. IRR Analysis: compute Cohen kappa and Pearson r between model_evaluations and human_graded_samples per rubric dimension, per model. Cost Benchmark: aggregate inference_seconds, estimated_kwh from DCGM metrics, hardware_depreciation_usd, produce cost_per_essay_usd versus commercial API pricing from static config.
target_output.system_components: nginx reverse proxy (TLS 1.3, JWT validation, ports 443 and 8501), FastAPI scoring service (port 8000, routers: /scoring-runs /results /models /rubrics /cost), Ollama LLM server (port 11434 localhost-only, serving Llama3-8B Q4_K_M and Llama3-70B Q4_K_M and Mistral7B Q4_K_M), Streamlit researcher dashboard (port 8501), SQLite database (WAL mode, tables: models and scoring_rubrics and scoring_runs and student_essays and model_evaluations and human_graded_samples and cost_benchmarks), Prometheus (scraping FastAPI /metrics and DCGM exporter port 9400), Grafana (GPU utilization and inference latency and cost dashboards), NVIDIA DCGM Exporter (GPU power draw and memory metrics).
target_output.workflows: Essay Scoring Run: researcher uploads anonymized essay TXT files and selects rubric JSON and target model from Streamlit UI; FastAPI strips document metadata and assigns pseudonymous IDs; scoring orchestrator dequeues each essay, constructs rubric-aware chain-of-thought prompt (system: grading role and rubric dimensions with point scales; user: essay text), submits to Ollama POST /api/generate with model tag; parsed Pydantic response (score_int and rationale per dimension) immediately checkpointed to model_evaluations with model_id NOT NULL; after final essay, aggregate Cohen kappa and Pearson r computed against human_graded_samples and appended to run record. Cost Benchmarking: post-run cost_benchmarks record created by querying Prometheus DCGM power metrics for the run duration window, computing estimated kWh, applying hardware depreciation share (server amortized cost divided by expected_lifespan_hours multiplied by run_duration_hours), dividing total by essay count; Streamlit cost page renders per-model cost_per_essay_usd bar chart versus commercial_api_cost_per_essay_usd from static pricing config file.
target_output.data_model: models (id UUID PK, name, ollama_tag, parameter_count_b INT, quantization_level VARCHAR). scoring_rubrics (id UUID PK, name, dimensions_json JSONB, max_score_per_dimension INT, created_by, created_at). scoring_runs (id UUID PK, model_id FK->models NOT NULL, rubric_id FK->scoring_rubrics, course_id, essay_count INT, completed_count INT, status ENUM queued/running/completed/failed, submitted_at, completed_at). student_essays (id UUID PK, run_id FK->scoring_runs, pseudonymous_student_id VARCHAR NOT NULL, anonymized_text TEXT, word_count INT, uploaded_at). model_evaluations (id UUID PK, run_id FK->scoring_runs, essay_id FK->student_essays, model_id FK->models NOT NULL, rubric_dimension VARCHAR, score_int INT, rationale_text TEXT, inference_latency_ms INT, prompt_tokens INT, completion_tokens INT). human_graded_samples (id UUID PK, essay_id FK->student_essays, rubric_dimension, human_score INT, grader_id). cost_benchmarks (id UUID PK, run_id FK->scoring_runs, model_id FK->models NOT NULL, total_essays INT, total_tokens INT, inference_seconds FLOAT, estimated_kwh FLOAT, hardware_depreciation_usd FLOAT, cost_per_essay_usd FLOAT, commercial_api_cost_per_essay_usd FLOAT).
target_output.api_design: FastAPI REST: POST /scoring-runs (multipart: essay files and model_id and rubric_id, returns run_id), GET /scoring-runs/{run_id} (status and progress percentage), GET /scoring-runs/{run_id}/results (per-essay scores by rubric dimension and IRR metrics), GET /scoring-runs/{run_id}/cost (cost_benchmark record with per-model cost comparison), GET /models (list available Ollama models with status), POST /rubrics (create rubric JSON with dimension definitions), GET /rubrics/{rubric_id}. All endpoints require Bearer JWT. Background: POST /scoring-runs triggers asyncio task that calls Ollama POST /api/generate for each essay.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Custom JWT with 8-hour expiry; nginx validates Authorization header via lua script before proxying to FastAPI. TLS 1.3 via Let's Encrypt certificate on nginx. University VPN is the only network path to the server. Essay files stripped of PDF author and creator metadata on ingest using PyMuPDF. Pseudonymous_student_id derived from HMAC-SHA256(irb_participant_code, HMAC_KEY) where HMAC_KEY stored in env file excluded from git and backups. Raw IRB participant code not persisted after ID generation. Audit log: every POST /scoring-runs and GET /results/{run_id} logged with researcher JWT subject, run_id, and timestamp to append-only audit.log. FERPA: essays are educational records; access restricted to researchers named on the active IRB protocol.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Hetzner AX102 bare-metal server (AMD Ryzen 9 7950X, RTX 4090 24GB VRAM, 64GB DDR5). Services managed as systemd units: fastapi.service, ollama.service, prometheus.service, grafana-server.service, streamlit.service, dcgm-exporter.service. nginx config routes /api/* to port 8000 (FastAPI) and / to port 8501 (Streamlit). GitHub Actions runs ruff and pytest on every PR with no automated deploy. Deployment is manual git pull main followed by systemctl restart fastapi streamlit on the research server via SSH. SQLite WAL mode with daily rsync backup to local external drive. Alembic migration applied manually before each deployment.
target_output.observability: Prometheus metrics from FastAPI /metrics endpoint: scoring_run_duration_seconds (histogram with model_id label), essay_inference_latency_ms (histogram with model_id label), active_scoring_runs (gauge), scoring_failures_total (counter with reason label). DCGM Exporter metrics: DCGM_FI_DEV_GPU_UTIL, DCGM_FI_DEV_POWER_USAGE, DCGM_FI_DEV_FB_USED. Grafana dashboard panels: GPU utilization timeline per scoring run, inference latency p50 and p95 per model, GPU memory headroom, cost accumulation per run. Structured JSON logs to /var/log/essay-scorer/ with 30-day file rotation. Grafana alert if GPU memory FB_USED exceeds 22GB (risk of OOM during 70B model inference).
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Hetzner AX102 bare-metal: approximately USD 180 per month. RTX 4090 amortized at USD 2000 over 36 months adds USD 56 per month hardware depreciation component. Estimated inference cost: USD 0.003 to 0.008 per essay for 8B model, USD 0.012 to 0.020 per essay for 70B model (including electricity at USD 0.15 per kWh and hardware depreciation), versus USD 0.04 to 0.12 per essay for commercial scoring APIs -- validating the research hypothesis at scale. No scaling planned; single-server baseline is the research design requirement.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (Weeks 1-6): FastAPI scoring service with Ollama integration for Llama 3-8B, SQLite schema with model_id isolation (ISS-001 fix applied), per-essay checkpoint writes in orchestrator (ISS-002 fix), Streamlit run configuration and status pages, JWT auth. Phase 2 (Weeks 7-10): IRR computation module (Cohen kappa and Pearson r), cost benchmark module with DCGM Prometheus metrics, Grafana dashboards, multi-model support (Llama 3-70B and Mistral 7B added to models table and Ollama). Phase 3 (Weeks 11-12): Cost benchmark dashboard in Streamlit with commercial API comparison, rubric library with pre-configured dimension sets for writing courses, automated accuracy and cost report export for paper submission.
target_output.development_guidelines: Python ruff linting enforced in CI. Pydantic models for all FastAPI request/response schemas with strict validation. pytest unit tests required for rubric prompt template construction, Ollama response JSON parser, IRR calculation (kappa and Pearson r), and cost benchmark computation. Target 80% line coverage on core logic modules. SQLAlchemy ORM only with no raw SQL strings. Alembic migration for every schema change. model_id must never be nullable in any evaluation record; enforce at ORM model level with nullable=False.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Llama 3-70B Q4_K_M inference latency (10-15 tokens per second on RTX 4090) makes large batches of long essays (1000-plus words, 500-plus essays) require overnight runs. Mitigation: enforce 1500-word cap on essay upload and note batch size recommendations in tool documentation. Risk: Ollama service OOM during 70B model run on very long essays. Mitigation: max_tokens cap at 512 completion tokens and word count validation at ingest. Tradeoff: SQLite over PostgreSQL simplifies single-server deployment but limits concurrent write access; acceptable since scoring runs are serialized through the asyncio queue. Tradeoff: Streamlit over React frontend reduces development time at the cost of limited interactive visualization options for complex IRR charts; mitigated by CSV export for researcher analysis in R or Python notebooks.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Selected Ollama over vLLM for simpler single-GPU setup; vLLM offers better throughput but requires more infrastructure configuration unsuitable for a research prototype timeline. Confirmed HMAC-SHA256 pseudonymization over random UUID assignment to allow cross-run linking of the same student essays without storing a raw identifier mapping table. IRB protocol scope confirmed to cover three introductory writing courses (ENGL 101, ENGL 102, COMM 150) for the initial paper submission dataset.

metadata.notes: Research prototype for open-source LLM essay scoring cost benchmarking; round 2 fixes model_id isolation in data schema to enable valid cross-model statistical comparison.

project_goal.value: Build a high-scale data pipeline ingesting clickstream and learning activity events from K-12 student browsers and mobile clients, computing real-time per-student per-course engagement metrics, and feeding a recommendation engine that surfaces next-best learning activities
project_goal.rationale: Core platform need: transform raw event streams into actionable engagement intelligence at millions-of-students scale

target_users.value: Internal data engineering and ML teams consuming engagement metrics and recommendation signals; student-facing product teams querying ClickHouse dashboards
target_users.rationale: Pipeline is internal-only; no direct student-facing UI in this service

project_class.rationale: Pure data ingestion, transformation, and serving pipeline with no user-facing application layer

capabilities.rationale: Combines real-time Flink streaming with nightly batch ML feature jobs and ClickHouse analytics serving

complexity_level.rationale: Millions of concurrent students; peak clickstream ingestion up to 500K events/sec during school-day hours

risk_level.rationale: Student PII in event streams; COPPA and FERPA obligations; no payment data or PHI

data_sensitivity.rationale: Clickstream events include student IDs and learning activity identifiers tied to real minors

external_exposure.rationale: Pipeline APIs and dashboards accessible only from internal VPC; no public endpoints

access_model.value: Service-account-based access for pipeline components; Cognito plus RBAC for internal dashboard consumers; no student direct access
access_model.rationale: Pipeline is a backend service; human access limited to data engineering and analytics roles

feature_scope.value: Kafka-based event ingestion from browser SDK and mobile SDK, Flink streaming jobs for session engagement scoring, ClickHouse OLAP for near-real-time queries, nightly batch feature-engineering jobs for recommendation model, Redis cache for low-latency recommendation serving, data quality monitoring and alerting
feature_scope.rationale: Full pipeline from raw events to served recommendations

mvp_scope.value: Kafka ingestion plus basic Flink engagement scoring plus ClickHouse query layer; recommendation feature jobs deferred to phase 2
mvp_scope.rationale: MVP delivers actionable engagement metrics without ML dependency

security_baseline.value: TLS 1.3 on all Kafka broker and Flink connections, student IDs pseudonymized at ingestion boundary via HMAC-SHA256 keyed hash, IAM roles for all EKS service accounts, no raw PII stored in hot tier beyond 30 days
security_baseline.rationale: COPPA and FERPA minimum controls for a pipeline processing student event data

frontend_stack.value: none -- data pipeline only; ClickHouse is queried directly by Grafana and internal BI tools
frontend_stack.rationale: No user-facing frontend in scope; Grafana provides pipeline observability dashboards

backend_stack.value: Apache Kafka 3.7 (MSK), Apache Flink 1.19 on EKS, Python 3.12 batch jobs via Argo Workflows
backend_stack.rationale: MSK removes broker ops overhead; Flink provides exactly-once stateful streaming; Argo handles DAG batch scheduling

data_platform.value: ClickHouse 24.x on EKS for OLAP hot tier, S3 with Apache Iceberg for cold archive, Redis 7 for recommendation serving cache
data_platform.rationale: ClickHouse delivers sub-second aggregation queries at billions of rows; Iceberg enables time-travel and schema evolution on cold data

hosting_target.value: AWS EKS (Kubernetes) in us-east-1 and us-west-2 for regional failover; MSK multi-AZ; ClickHouse sharded cluster on EKS
hosting_target.rationale: EKS gives Kubernetes-native scheduling for Flink jobs and batch Argo workflows; multi-region for availability

privacy_retention_policy.value: Raw clickstream events retained 30 days in Kafka; pseudonymized engagement metrics retained 2 years in ClickHouse hot tier; cold archive in S3 Iceberg indefinitely for model retraining; COPPA deletion requests fulfilled within 45 days across all tiers
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: COPPA and FERPA retention obligations; ML team needs multi-year data for retraining

future_scope.value: Real-time A/B testing framework feeding engagement signals back to content teams; cross-platform identity graph linking browser and mobile sessions; LLM-based anomaly explanation for engagement drops
future_scope.rationale: Year 2 roadmap expansions building on the established pipeline

constraints.value: Peak ingestion must handle 500K events/sec; p99 end-to-end latency from event emit to ClickHouse queryable within 90 seconds; budget ceiling $25K/month AWS spend; must comply with COPPA and FERPA
constraints.rationale: SLA and compliance constraints agreed with product and legal

observability_baseline.value: Grafana plus Prometheus for Flink and Kafka metrics; custom pipeline health dashboard tracking lag, throughput, and DLQ depth; PagerDuty for consumer lag >60s and DLQ spike alerts
observability_baseline.rationale: Standard observability stack already deployed on EKS cluster

execution_preference.value: Two-week sprints; pipeline components deployed independently via Helm charts; schema changes require a migration review in engineering sync
execution_preference.rationale: Team workflow and deployment safety preference

compliance_context.value: COPPA: verifiable parental consent required before collecting data from students under 13; data minimization enforced at SDK layer. FERPA: student educational records accessible only to authorized school personnel; data sharing with third parties prohibited without consent. Internal DPA executed with school district partners.
compliance_context.rationale: Platform serves K-12 minors; both COPPA and FERPA apply to learning activity data

project.goal: High-throughput clickstream and learning activity pipeline with real-time engagement scoring and ML recommendation feature serving
project.target_users: Internal data engineering, ML, and analytics teams
project.timeline: MVP in Q2; full recommendation pipeline by Q4

backend.runtime: Kafka 3.7 (MSK) + Flink 1.19 on EKS + Python 3.12 batch jobs
backend.framework: Argo Workflows for batch DAGs; Flink DataStream API for streaming jobs
backend.api: Internal gRPC endpoints for recommendation serving; no public HTTP API

security.auth: Service accounts with IAM roles for EKS; Cognito for human dashboard access
security.transport: TLS 1.3 on MSK, mTLS between Flink and ClickHouse
security.compliance: COPPA parental consent enforcement at SDK boundary; FERPA access controls on ClickHouse views

data.db: ClickHouse 24.x sharded on EKS (hot tier), S3 Iceberg (cold tier)
data.cache: Redis 7 Cluster for recommendation serving; in-memory Flink state backed by RocksDB

devops.ci: GitHub Actions building Docker images plus Helm chart validation; Argo CD for GitOps deployment
devops.hosting: EKS us-east-1 plus us-west-2 active-passive; MSK multi-AZ; ClickHouse 3-shard 2-replica

open_questions.parental_consent_token: How are COPPA consent tokens propagated through the event stream?

confirmed_decisions.pseudonymization: HMAC-SHA256 student ID hashing at Kafka producer boundary
confirmed_decisions.cold_archive: S3 Iceberg for schema-evolution-safe historical storage

reasoner_reviews.product: The pipeline correctly prioritises per-student per-course engagement scoring as the core value delivery. Surfacing next-best activities via Redis-backed recommendations closes the feedback loop from raw events to learner-visible outcomes. The phasing -- metrics first, recommendations second -- de-risks ML dependency on the MVP timeline.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Flink 1.19 with RocksDB state backend is the right choice for stateful session-window aggregation at this event rate. The ClickHouse sharded cluster needs careful partition key design: sharding by student_id risks hotspots during national test events; a composite shard key on cityHash64(course_id) distributes write pressure more evenly. The ISS-002 concern about ClickHouse partition key remains unresolved from round 2.
reasoner_reviews.security: Pseudonymization at the Kafka producer boundary is the correct control point -- raw student IDs must not reach Kafka brokers or downstream consumers. The ISS-001 COPPA consent token propagation gap was partially addressed in round 2 but the deletion propagation path through ClickHouse and Iceberg still lacks a concrete mechanism. This must be specified in round 3.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: The $25K/month ceiling is achievable with MSK Provisioned at 30 broker-units plus a 9-node ClickHouse cluster on m6i.4xlarge, but only if Flink job parallelism is tuned to avoid over-provisioning EKS node groups during off-peak hours. Recommend Karpenter for node autoscaling.

backend.service_design: Three logical layers: (1) Ingestion layer -- Kafka MSK with 128-partition clickstream_events topic and 64-partition activity_completions topic. (2) Streaming layer -- Flink jobs: SessionEngagementJob (session-window per student_id, computes dwell time, interaction depth, completion rate per activity), MetricPublisherJob (writes engagement_metrics rows to ClickHouse via JDBC sink). (3) Batch layer -- Argo Workflows DAG: nightly FeatureEngineeringJob aggregates 30-day engagement windows per (student_id_hash, course_id), writes feature vectors to S3 Iceberg feature store; RecommendationScoringJob runs collaborative-filter model, writes top-5 activity IDs per student to Redis.
backend.api_patterns: gRPC RecommendationService (GetNextActivities(student_id, course_id) returns ActivityList) served by a Go microservice reading from Redis; internal HTTP endpoint /metrics/engagement?student_id=&course_id= reading ClickHouse via query proxy; no public-facing APIs
backend.failure_handling: Kafka DLQ topic clickstream_events_dlq for deserialization failures; Flink checkpoints every 30s to S3; Argo Workflows retry policy 3x with exponential backoff; ClickHouse write failures route to a S3 buffer file consumed by a reconciliation job
backend.scaling_notes: Flink TaskManager autoscaling via KEDA Kafka consumer lag metric; ClickHouse horizontal shard expansion by adding shard pairs to the cluster; MSK partition count pre-scaled to 128 to avoid partition reassignment under load
backend.background_jobs: Nightly feature engineering at 02:00 UTC; weekly model retraining trigger via Argo cron workflow; daily COPPA deletion sweep job checking deletion_requests table against all storage tiers

security.auth_design: EKS service accounts with IRSA (IAM Roles for Service Accounts) scoped per pipeline component; Cognito user pool with RBAC for Grafana and ClickHouse proxy access by human analysts; no student-facing auth in pipeline scope
security.secrets_management: AWS Secrets Manager for ClickHouse credentials and HMAC pseudonymization key; External Secrets Operator syncing secrets into EKS; pseudonymization key rotated every 90 days with dual-key overlap window to avoid re-hashing historical data
security.privacy_controls: Student IDs hashed at Kafka producer SDK before transmission; consent_token field attached to every event; Flink enrichment job validates consent_token against consent_registry before emitting to engagement_metrics topic; events without valid consent dropped to consent_violations_dlq for COPPA audit
security.audit_and_logging_controls: ClickHouse access audit log capturing analyst queries with student_id dimension via ClickHouse query log; S3 access logging on Iceberg bucket; PII access events forwarded to CloudTrail

data.entities: clickstream_events (event_id UUID, student_id_hash VARCHAR, course_id UUID, activity_id UUID, event_type ENUM page_view/interaction/video_play/quiz_attempt/completion, session_id UUID, client_ts TIMESTAMP, server_ts TIMESTAMP, consent_token VARCHAR, device_type ENUM browser/mobile). engagement_metrics (student_id_hash, course_id, activity_id, session_engagement_score FLOAT, dwell_seconds INT, interaction_count INT, completed BOOL, computed_at TIMESTAMP) stored in ClickHouse partitioned by toYYYYMM(computed_at). deletion_requests (request_id UUID, original_student_id VARCHAR, student_id_hash VARCHAR, requested_at TIMESTAMP, status ENUM pending/completed, completed_at TIMESTAMP) in PostgreSQL control plane.
data.storage_design: ClickHouse 3-shard 2-replica cluster sharded by cityHash64(course_id) % 3 to distribute course-level write bursts; engagement_metrics table uses ReplicatedMergeTree with TTL 730 days; S3 Iceberg cold archive partitioned by event_date and course_id; Redis Cluster 6-node for recommendation cache with TTL 24 hours per key
data.retention_and_deletion: Raw Kafka events retained 30 days via MSK retention policy; ClickHouse engagement_metrics TTL 2 years; S3 Iceberg cold data retained indefinitely for model retraining; COPPA deletion requests processed by daily sweep job within 45 days
data.schema_notes: All student identifiers stored as HMAC-SHA256 hashes; consent_token validated against external consent_registry service before downstream storage; ClickHouse schema migrations via clickhouse-migrations tool with backward-compatible column additions only

devops.deployment_topology: EKS cluster per region (us-east-1 primary, us-west-2 passive); MSK multi-AZ 6-broker cluster; ClickHouse pods on dedicated m6i.4xlarge node group with local NVMe; Flink jobs deployed as Kubernetes-native FlinkDeployment CRDs via Flink Kubernetes Operator; Argo Workflows on shared node pool
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: lint Python/Go, run Flink unit tests via flink-testing framework, build Docker images, push ECR, validate Helm charts with helm lint plus kubeval; Argo CD GitOps for all EKS workload deployments; Helm chart per pipeline component (kafka-consumer, flink-streaming, batch-jobs, clickhouse-proxy)
devops.observability_stack: Prometheus scraping Kafka JMX exporter, Flink metrics reporter, ClickHouse exporter, and KEDA metrics; Grafana dashboards: Pipeline Throughput (events/sec by topic), Consumer Lag (per consumer group), Flink Job Health (checkpoint duration, restarts, backpressure), ClickHouse Query Performance (p50/p99 query latency, merge queue depth); PagerDuty for consumer lag >60s, DLQ depth >1000, Flink job failure
devops.rollback_strategy: Flink jobs rolled back via FlinkDeployment spec revert in Git plus Argo CD sync; ClickHouse schema rollback blocked by MergeTree immutability -- new columns only, no column removals in running pipelines; Kafka consumer group offset reset to checkpoint timestamp for event replay; Helm rollback for batch job containers
devops.cost_controls: Karpenter spot-instance node groups for Flink TaskManagers (60% spot); MSK Provisioned sized to p95 throughput; ClickHouse reserved instances on 1-year term for baseline nodes; S3 Intelligent-Tiering for Iceberg cold archive; estimated $22K/month at sustained load

ISS-001.title: COPPA deletion propagation path unspecified across ClickHouse and S3 Iceberg tiers
ISS-001.detail: Round 2 plan acknowledged deletion requests but did not specify the mechanism for removing pseudonymized rows from ClickHouse TTL tables and issuing S3 Iceberg DELETE predicates within the 45-day COPPA window. The deletion_requests table was added but no job or procedure was defined.

history.note: COPPA deletion requirement identified by security reviewer
deletion_requests table added to schema but processing job not specified

ISS-002.title: ClickHouse shard key choice creates hotspot risk during national assessment events
ISS-002.detail: Round 2 used student_id_hash as the ClickHouse shard key. During national standardized test windows, millions of students in the same cohort courses write simultaneously, causing shards keyed to those course_ids to receive disproportionate write load.

history.note: Architect reasoner flagged hotspot risk; no change made in round 2 plan

ISS-003.title: Consent token validation adds synchronous latency to high-throughput Flink path
ISS-003.detail: Round 1 proposed synchronous consent_registry lookup inside the Flink enrichment job. Round 2 resolved this by pre-loading consent tokens into a Flink broadcast state refreshed every 5 minutes, eliminating per-event network calls.

history.note: Synchronous consent lookup identified as throughput bottleneck
Broadcast state pattern adopted; resolved

focus_issues.title: COPPA deletion propagation path unspecified across ClickHouse and S3 Iceberg tiers
focus_issues.detail: Daily deletion sweep job and S3 Iceberg DELETE predicate mechanism must be fully specified in round 3 plan sections: data_model, workflows, security_and_compliance.

focus_issues.title: ClickHouse shard key choice creates hotspot risk during national assessment events
focus_issues.detail: Shard key must be changed from student_id_hash to cityHash64(course_id) % 3 composite and documented in data_model and architecture_overview.

latest_recommendations: Specify the COPPA deletion sweep job mechanism end-to-end: how deletion_requests are populated, how the sweep job locates ClickHouse rows by student_id_hash, and how S3 Iceberg DELETE predicates are issued
Change ClickHouse shard key from student_id_hash to cityHash64(course_id) to distribute assessment-day write bursts across shards

target_output.thinking_summary: Round 3 must resolve ISS-001 (COPPA deletion sweep) and ISS-002 (ClickHouse shard hotspot). For ISS-001 I will fully specify the daily Argo deletion sweep job end-to-end: query deletion_requests, execute ClickHouse ALTER TABLE DELETE WHERE student_id_hash IN batch, issue S3 Iceberg overwrite DELETE via PyIceberg predicate, update deletion_requests.status to completed. For ISS-002 I will change the ClickHouse shard key from student_id_hash to cityHash64(course_id) % 3 and document the one-time rebalancing procedure. Both fixes surface in data_model, workflows, security_and_compliance, and deployment sections.

fix_report.action_taken: Specified the daily COPPA deletion sweep Argo Workflow with three steps: (1) query deletion_requests WHERE status=pending from PostgreSQL control plane, (2) execute batched ClickHouse ALTER TABLE engagement_metrics DELETE WHERE student_id_hash IN (hashes) and ALTER TABLE clickstream_events_archive DELETE WHERE student_id_hash IN (hashes), (3) issue PyIceberg table.delete(EqualTo('student_id_hash', hash)) predicate on S3 Iceberg cold archive, then UPDATE deletion_requests SET status=completed. Documented in workflows, data_model, security_and_compliance, and deployment_and_operations sections.
fix_report.expected_outcome: COPPA deletion requests processed end-to-end within 45-day window across Kafka (TTL handles automatically), ClickHouse hot tier, and S3 Iceberg cold tier

fix_report.action_taken: Changed ClickHouse shard key from student_id_hash to cityHash64(course_id) % 3. This distributes writes across shards by course rather than by student, preventing concentration during national assessment windows when all students in a cohort course write simultaneously. Added one-time rebalancing procedure using SYSTEM STOP MERGES, INSERT INTO new table SELECT from old table, DROP old table. Documented in data_model and architecture_overview.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Write throughput distributed evenly across 3 ClickHouse shards during peak assessment events; no single shard exceeds 45% of cluster write IOPS

target_output.title: K-12 Learning Platform Clickstream Pipeline v3
target_output.executive_summary: A COPPA/FERPA-compliant Kafka plus Flink streaming pipeline on AWS EKS (us-east-1/us-west-2) ingesting up to 500K student clickstream events per second from browser and mobile SDKs, computing per-student per-course session engagement scores via Flink 1.19 session windows, storing metrics in a sharded ClickHouse 24.x cluster for sub-second analytics, and serving next-best-activity recommendations from Redis 7. A fully specified daily COPPA deletion sweep Argo Workflow propagates deletion requests through ClickHouse, S3 Iceberg cold archive, and Redis within the 45-day compliance window.
target_output.architecture_overview: MSK Kafka 3.7 (128-partition clickstream_events topic, 64-partition activity_completions topic) receives HMAC-pseudonymized events from browser and mobile SDKs. Flink 1.19 SessionEngagementJob consumes clickstream_events, computes dwell time, interaction depth, and completion rate per (student_id_hash, activity_id) in 30-minute session windows with RocksDB state backend, and emits engagement_metrics rows to ClickHouse via JDBC sink. ClickHouse 24.x 3-shard 2-replica cluster sharded by cityHash64(course_id) % 3 distributes write load evenly across school-day peaks and national assessment events, resolving the ISS-002 hotspot concern. Nightly Argo Workflows DAG runs FeatureEngineeringJob aggregating 30-day engagement windows into feature vectors written to S3 Iceberg, and RecommendationScoringJob writing top-5 activity IDs per student to Redis hash keys. Daily COPPA deletion sweep Argo Workflow propagates deletion requests from PostgreSQL control plane through ClickHouse ALTER TABLE DELETE and S3 Iceberg PyIceberg DELETE predicates within 45 days.
target_output.technology_stack: Streaming: Apache Kafka 3.7 on MSK multi-AZ, Apache Flink 1.19 on EKS via Flink Kubernetes Operator with RocksDB state backend and S3 checkpoints. Batch: Python 3.12 jobs orchestrated by Argo Workflows 3.5 on EKS. OLAP storage: ClickHouse 24.x 3-shard 2-replica on m6i.4xlarge EKS node group with local NVMe. Cold archive: S3 with Apache Iceberg 1.5 plus PyIceberg for DELETE predicates. Recommendation serving: Redis 7 Cluster 6-node. Orchestration: Argo CD for GitOps, Karpenter for EKS node autoscaling. Observability: Prometheus plus Grafana, Kafka JMX exporter, Flink metrics reporter, PagerDuty. CI: GitHub Actions. IaC: Terraform plus Helm.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Clickstream Ingestion: Browser SDK and React Native mobile SDK emit page_view, interaction, video_play, quiz_attempt, and completion events with HMAC-hashed student_id and consent_token to Kafka clickstream_events topic. Session Engagement Scoring: Flink SessionEngagementJob groups events by (student_id_hash, session_id) in 30-minute gap-based session windows, computes dwell_seconds, interaction_count, completion flag, emits engagement_metrics rows. Engagement Analytics: ClickHouse serves course-level engagement dashboards with p99 query latency under 500ms. Recommendation Feature Engineering: Nightly Argo DAG aggregates 30-day rolling engagement vectors and writes Parquet feature files to S3 Iceberg. Recommendation Serving: Redis hash keys store top-5 next-activity IDs; gRPC RecommendationService serves them with p99 under 10ms. COPPA Compliance: Daily deletion sweep resolves pending deletion_requests across all storage tiers within 45 days.
target_output.system_components: MSK Kafka cluster (6 brokers, 3 AZs, 128 partitions for clickstream_events, DLQ topic clickstream_events_dlq). Flink EKS deployment (JobManager 2-replica, TaskManager autoscaled 4-20 pods via KEDA on Kafka consumer lag metric). ClickHouse EKS cluster (3 shards, 2 replicas each, dedicated m6i.4xlarge node group with 900GB local NVMe, Keeper coordination). S3 Iceberg cold archive (PyIceberg catalog backed by AWS Glue). Argo Workflows EKS deployment for nightly batch DAGs. Redis 7 Cluster (6-node m6g.large). gRPC RecommendationService (Go, 3 replicas). PostgreSQL control plane (RDS t3.medium) for deletion_requests and consent_registry. External Secrets Operator for AWS Secrets Manager injection.
target_output.workflows: Clickstream Ingestion and Engagement Scoring: Browser SDK captures student interaction events, attaches HMAC-SHA256(student_id, secret_key) hash and consent_token, batches 50 events per POST to Kafka REST proxy. Kafka routes events to SessionEngagementJob TaskManagers. Flink validates consent_token against broadcast consent state (refreshed every 5 minutes from consent_registry), drops invalid events to consent_violations_dlq, computes session window aggregates, writes engagement_metrics to ClickHouse via async JDBC sink with 200ms buffer. ClickHouse merges and indexes rows; Grafana dashboards reflect updated engagement within 90 seconds of event emission. Nightly Recommendation Refresh: Argo Workflows DAG triggers at 02:00 UTC. FeatureEngineeringJob queries ClickHouse for 30-day engagement windows, transforms into feature vectors, writes Parquet files to S3 Iceberg. RecommendationScoringJob loads feature vectors and pre-trained model, scores activity candidates, writes top-5 activity_ids per student to Redis HSET student_id_hash:course_id with 24-hour TTL. COPPA Deletion Sweep: Daily at 03:00 UTC, Argo Workflow step 1 queries SELECT original_student_id, student_id_hash FROM deletion_requests WHERE status=pending from PostgreSQL control plane. Step 2 executes batched ClickHouse ALTER TABLE engagement_metrics DELETE WHERE student_id_hash IN (hash_list) with FINAL modifier to ensure synchronous mutation execution. Step 3 calls PyIceberg table.delete(In('student_id_hash', hash_list)) on the S3 Iceberg cold archive, triggering a rewrite of affected Iceberg data files. Step 4 executes DEL on Redis keys matching student_id_hash:* pattern. Step 5 marks all processed requests completed with completed_at timestamp. Total sweep runtime under 30 minutes for batches of 10K deletion requests; COPPA 45-day SLA met.
target_output.data_model: clickstream_events (event_id UUID, student_id_hash VARCHAR(64) HMAC-SHA256, course_id UUID, activity_id UUID, event_type ENUM page_view/interaction/video_play/quiz_attempt/completion, session_id UUID, consent_token VARCHAR(128), device_type ENUM browser/mobile, client_ts DateTime64, server_ts DateTime64) -- Kafka topic retention 30 days; archived subset in S3 Iceberg cold tier. engagement_metrics (student_id_hash VARCHAR(64), course_id UUID, activity_id UUID, session_engagement_score Float32, dwell_seconds UInt32, interaction_count UInt16, completed UInt8, computed_at DateTime) -- ClickHouse ReplicatedMergeTree sharded by cityHash64(course_id) % 3 (ISS-002 fix: changed from student_id_hash to prevent assessment-day hotspots), partitioned by toYYYYMM(computed_at), TTL computed_at + INTERVAL 2 YEAR. activity_completions (completion_id UUID, student_id_hash VARCHAR(64), activity_id UUID, course_id UUID, score Float32, completed_at DateTime) -- ClickHouse ReplicatedMergeTree, same shard key. recommendation_signals (Redis hash key student_id_hash:course_id, value JSON array of top-5 activity_ids, TTL 24h). deletion_requests (request_id UUID PK, original_student_id VARCHAR(256), student_id_hash VARCHAR(64), requested_at TIMESTAMP, status ENUM pending/completed, completed_at TIMESTAMP NULLABLE, requester_id VARCHAR(128)) -- PostgreSQL control plane. consent_registry (student_id_hash VARCHAR(64) PK, consent_granted BOOL, consent_version VARCHAR(16), granted_at TIMESTAMP, parent_email_hash VARCHAR(64)) -- PostgreSQL control plane, loaded into Flink broadcast state every 5 minutes. ClickHouse shard rebalancing procedure: SYSTEM STOP MERGES on all shards, INSERT INTO engagement_metrics_new SELECT * FROM engagement_metrics using cityHash64(course_id) distribution key, DROP TABLE engagement_metrics, RENAME engagement_metrics_new TO engagement_metrics.
target_output.api_design: Internal gRPC: RecommendationService.GetNextActivities(student_id_hash STRING, course_id STRING) returns ActivityList{activity_ids: repeated string, model_version: string, served_at: timestamp}; served by Go service reading Redis hash. Internal HTTP (ClickHouse proxy, VPC-only): GET /v1/engagement?student_id_hash=&course_id=&from=&to= returns engagement_metrics rows as JSON; GET /v1/engagement/course-summary?course_id=&date= returns aggregated course-level stats. Internal HTTP (control plane): POST /v1/deletion-requests {original_student_id, requester_id} inserts into deletion_requests table; GET /v1/deletion-requests/:id returns status. All HTTP endpoints authenticated via Cognito JWT validated at ALB level; gRPC authenticated via mTLS client certificates.
target_output.security_and_compliance: COPPA controls: parental consent_token attached to every event at SDK layer; Flink broadcast-state validation drops unconsented events before ClickHouse storage; daily Argo deletion sweep processes deletion_requests end-to-end (ClickHouse ALTER TABLE DELETE with FINAL modifier, S3 Iceberg PyIceberg DELETE predicate, Redis DEL) within 45-day SLA; consent_registry stores parent_email_hash and consent_version for audit. FERPA controls: engagement_metrics and recommendation_signals accessible only to authorized school-district service accounts and internal analyst roles via Cognito RBAC; ClickHouse row-level security policies restrict analyst access to their district's course_id set; audit log captures all analyst queries via ClickHouse system.query_log forwarded to S3. Pseudonymization: HMAC-SHA256(student_id, rotation_key) at SDK boundary; key stored in AWS Secrets Manager, rotated every 90 days with 7-day overlap window; historical hash mapping retained in deletion_requests for sweep job. Transport security: TLS 1.3 on MSK client connections, mTLS between Flink JDBC sink and ClickHouse, TLS on all EKS ingress.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: EKS clusters: us-east-1 primary (active), us-west-2 passive (Argo CD mirrored but Flink jobs paused until failover). MSK: 6-broker multi-AZ provisioned cluster, 30 broker-units, replication factor 3. ClickHouse: 3-shard 2-replica pods on dedicated m6i.4xlarge node group, PodAntiAffinity ensures replicas on different AZs; local NVMe mounted at /var/lib/clickhouse. Flink: FlinkDeployment CRD deployed via Flink Kubernetes Operator; TaskManagers autoscaled by KEDA targeting Kafka consumer lag under 5000 events; checkpoints to S3 every 30 seconds; savepoints taken before any job upgrade. Argo Workflows: nightly batch DAGs (02:00 UTC feature engineering, 03:00 UTC COPPA deletion sweep) on spot node group with Karpenter; retry policy 3x exponential backoff; PagerDuty alert on DAG failure within 5 minutes. CI/CD: GitHub Actions lints Python and Go, runs Flink unit tests via MiniCluster harness, validates Helm charts, builds and pushes Docker images to ECR; Argo CD syncs manifests on merge to main with manual promotion gate for production. Secrets: External Secrets Operator pulls HMAC key, ClickHouse credentials, and consent_registry DSN from AWS Secrets Manager into EKS Secrets with 90-day rotation schedule. Schema changes: ClickHouse column additions deployed via clickhouse-migrations during maintenance window; shard key changes require full rebalancing runbook (SYSTEM STOP MERGES, INSERT INTO new table, DROP old table, RENAME).
target_output.observability: Prometheus scrapes Kafka JMX exporter (messages_in_per_sec, consumer_lag per group), Flink metrics reporter (num_records_in_per_second, checkpoint_duration, num_restarts, back_pressured_time_ms_per_second), ClickHouse exporter (query_duration_ms p50/p99, merge_queue_size, parts_count), KEDA metrics for TaskManager replica count. Grafana dashboards: Pipeline Throughput (events/sec by Kafka topic), Consumer Lag Health (per consumer group with 60s SLA threshold), Flink Job Health (checkpoint success rate, restart frequency, backpressure heatmap), ClickHouse Performance (query latency p99 by query pattern, merge pressure, replication lag), Deletion Sweep Status (pending deletion_requests count, last sweep duration, overdue requests by age). PagerDuty alerts: consumer lag over 60s (P2), DLQ depth over 1000 events (P2), Flink job failure (P1), ClickHouse p99 query latency over 2s (P3), deletion sweep overdue over 40 days (P1 compliance alert), consent validation rejection rate over 0.1% (P2).
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Estimated $22K/month: MSK Provisioned $4.2K (30 broker-units), EKS worker nodes $7.8K (Flink TaskManagers 60% spot via Karpenter, ClickHouse on-demand reserved 1-year term), Redis Cluster $1.2K, RDS control plane $0.3K, S3 plus data transfer $2.1K, ECR plus miscellaneous $0.9K, reserved instance discount $5.5K offset. Scaling headroom: Flink TaskManagers autoscale to 20 pods handling 800K events/sec before MSK partition count is the bottleneck; partition expansion to 256 requires controlled reassignment during off-peak. ClickHouse horizontal scale by adding shard pair with rebalancing; vertical scale to r6i.4xlarge if MergeTree merge queue sustains over 100 parts.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 -- MVP Ingestion and Engagement Metrics (10 weeks): Deploy MSK cluster and browser/mobile SDK event emission with HMAC pseudonymization. Implement Flink SessionEngagementJob with consent broadcast-state validation. Stand up ClickHouse cluster with cityHash64(course_id) shard key. Deliver engagement dashboards in Grafana. Implement COPPA deletion sweep Argo Workflow end-to-end including PyIceberg DELETE. Establish Prometheus plus Grafana observability and PagerDuty compliance alerts. Phase 2 -- Recommendation Pipeline (8 weeks): Implement Argo Workflows nightly FeatureEngineeringJob and RecommendationScoringJob. Deploy Redis Cluster for recommendation serving. Deliver gRPC RecommendationService consumed by product team. Tune collaborative-filter model on first engagement data. Phase 3 -- Scale Hardening and Multi-Region (6 weeks): Activate us-west-2 passive cluster with MSK MirrorMaker 2 replication. Implement Flink savepoint-based job upgrade procedure. Add ClickHouse row-level security district isolation policies. Tune Karpenter spot instance interruption handling for Flink TaskManagers.
target_output.development_guidelines: All Kafka schema changes require Avro schema registry compatibility check before deployment. Flink job upgrades must produce a savepoint from the running job before deploying new version. ClickHouse schema changes limited to backward-compatible column additions during pipeline operation; shard key changes require full rebalancing runbook execution. COPPA deletion sweep changes require legal sign-off before deployment. Python batch jobs use pytest with over 85% coverage gate in CI. Go gRPC service uses golangci-lint and table-driven unit tests for recommendation cache reads. Feature flags control new Flink job variants before full rollout.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: ClickHouse ALTER TABLE DELETE mutations are asynchronous by default and may take minutes to propagate on heavily loaded shards, potentially causing a deletion_requests entry to remain pending beyond 45 days. Mitigation: FINAL modifier in deletion sweep forces synchronous mutation execution; compliance PagerDuty alert fires at 40 days. Risk: HMAC key rotation invalidates historical hash lookups in deletion sweep -- old hashes computed with previous key will not match new key output. Mitigation: deletion_requests stores student_id_hash computed at request time, so sweep re-uses the stored hash rather than recomputing with current key. Tradeoff: cityHash64(course_id) shard key distributes write load but causes hot reads on ClickHouse when a single popular course is queried by many analysts simultaneously; caching at Grafana and proxy layer mitigates. Tradeoff: Flink RocksDB state backend adds JVM memory pressure versus heap state; accepted for incremental checkpoint performance at 500K events/sec throughput.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Selected cityHash64(course_id) % 3 over student_id_hash as ClickHouse shard key to prevent assessment-day hotspots -- confirmed with data engineering team. Chose PyIceberg over Spark for S3 Iceberg DELETE predicates in deletion sweep because PyIceberg runs as a lightweight Python Argo step without a Spark cluster dependency. Confirmed HMAC overlap window of 7 days during key rotation to allow deletion sweep to resolve requests submitted during the rotation window. Decided deletion sweep runs daily at 03:00 UTC to batch ClickHouse mutations and minimize merge pressure.

metadata.notes: Round 3 revision resolving COPPA deletion sweep mechanism (ISS-001) and ClickHouse shard hotspot (ISS-002) for a 500K events/sec K-12 learning analytics pipeline.
